text:
SUMMARY:
This PR touches 124 files to bring Spanner PostgreSQL dialect test compatibility to life. Core changes include: Docker emulator setup for PostgreSQL dialect, new SpannerPostgreSQLDialect overrides (locking, check constraints, dual table, type handling), scalar subquery emulation for UPDATE statements when FROM clause is unsupported, extensible LIKE escape/pattern hook methods in AbstractSqlAstTranslator, and wide-ranging test annotations plus entity naming fixes to resolve `$` character incompatibilities.

POINTS:
- [hibernate-community-dialects/src/main/java/org/hibernate/community/dialect/SpannerPostgreSQLDialect.java:518-528] Missing `@Override` on `appendDateTimeLiteral(SqlAppender, Calendar, TemporalType, TimeZone)`. The sibling TemporalAccessor and Date overloads both carry `@Override`, and this Calendar overload clearly intends to override `Dialect.appendDateTimeLiteral(Calendar, ...)` (declared at Dialect.java:6068). Add the annotation for consistency and compile-time safety.

- [hibernate-community-dialects/src/main/java/org/hibernate/community/dialect/sql/ast/SpannerPostgreSQLSqlAstTranslator.java:30-38] `renderDmlTargetTableExpression` duplicates the base method body instead of calling `super`. Every other dialect override (PostgreSQLSqlAstTranslator:131, CockroachSqlAstTranslator:114, SpannerSqlAstTranslator:182) calls `super.renderDmlTargetTableExpression(tableReference)` first, then appends custom behavior. Call `super` so this override won't silently miss future base-method enhancements.

- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/sql/ast/spi/AbstractSqlAstTranslator.java:1121-1185] `createScalarSubquery` and `renderSetClauseEmulateScalarSubqueries` follow the established pattern from `determineWhereClauseRestrictionWithJoinEmulation` (same `QuerySpec` + dual-table + join-copy approach). Sound design — just verify the generated SQL is valid when the update has no joins (the subquery would select only the assigned value FROM dual, which is correct).

- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/sql/ast/spi/AbstractSqlAstTranslator.java:8241-8267] New `renderEscapeCharacter` and `renderLikePattern` hooks follow the class's existing extensibility conventions. Note: PostgreSQLSqlAstTranslator overrides `renderLikePredicate` to append `escape ''` when no escape is provided, but does NOT override `renderCaseInsensitiveLikeEmulation` — so case-insensitive LIKE in standard PostgreSQL still lacks that fix (pre-existing gap, not introduced by this PR).

- [hibernate-community-dialects/src/main/java/org/hibernate/community/dialect/SpannerPostgreSQLDialect.java:120-125] `SPANNER_LOCKING_SUPPORT` uses `NoLockingSupport.NO_LOCKING_SUPPORT` with a TODO, yet `getForUpdateString()` still returns `" for update"`. Confirm these methods won't be invoked when locking support reports no-locking, or override them to throw `UnsupportedOperationException` for defensive consistency.

- [docker_db.sh:1568-1631] `spanner_emulator` now installs `gcloud` via curl and configures the emulator instance/database/timezone. The curl-based gcloud install adds a network dependency and could slow CI; consider caching the SDK or documenting this as an explicit CI prerequisite.

- [hibernate-community-dialects/src/main/java/org/hibernate/community/dialect/SpannerPostgreSQLDialect.java:195-280] Three `getCheckCondition` overrides build OR-based check constraints with manual `'` escaping via `value.replace("'", "''")`. This is correct for single-quote escaping, but backslashes and control characters could still cause issues — consider whether a centralized string-escaping utility would be safer here.

- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/dialect/function/SpannerConcatFunction.java:34-46] `render` produces `concat((arg1)::text,(arg2)::text,...)`. Verify Spanner's CONCAT function actually needs the `::text` casts for all argument types — if inputs are already STRING, the casts are redundant.

- [hibernate-core/src/test/java/org/hibernate/orm/test/annotations/basic/ListOfStringTest.java and ~30 other test files] Widespread `@Entity(name = "...")` additions avoid `$` in entity names (e.g., `OneToManyRefColumnNameTest$That` → `That`). This works but scatters naming workarounds across many test classes. A Spanner-specific naming strategy override might centralize this concern.

SUMMARY:
This PR adds a new InterSystems IRIS dialect with 5 files covering the dialect, SQL AST translator, log function, identity column support, and limit handler. Implementation follows Hibernate conventions, but the dialect is missing registration in `CommunityDatabase` and `CommunityDialectSelector`, making it undiscoverable at runtime. The `datetimeFormat()` method also has a replacement ordering bug that breaks fractional-second format strings.

POINTS:
- [InterSystemsIRISDialect.java:NEW] **Missing dialect registration** — The dialect class is not registered in `CommunityDatabase.java` (enum) or `CommunityDialectSelector.java` (switch statement). Without these entries, Hibernate cannot auto-resolve this dialect. Follow how `CacheDialect` and other community dialects are registered. This blocks the dialect from being usable.
- [InterSystemsIRISDialect.java:775-820] **`datetimeFormat()` has broken replacement order for fractional seconds** — The `Replacer` processes replacements sequentially in registration order. `.replace("ss", "SS")` is registered BEFORE the fractional-second patterns (`.replace("SSSSSS", "FF6")`, etc.). When input is `"ssssss"`, the first two chars match and become `"SS"`, leaving `"SSssss"` which no longer matches `"SSSSSS"`. So `"yyyy-MM-dd HH:mm:ss.SSS"` becomes `"YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.SSS"` instead of `"YYYY-MM-DD HH24:MI:SS.FF3"`. Fractional-second patterns must be registered BEFORE `ss → SS`. (OracleDialect.java lines 1672-1681 has the same bug.)
- [InterSystemsIRISDialect.java:185] **Missing `@Override` annotation** — `supportsSubqueryOnMutatingTable()` overrides `Dialect.java:4494` but lacks `@Override`. This catches API changes at compile time.
- [InterSystemsIRISDialect.java:732] **Misleading comment on `getFractionalSecondPrecisionInNanos()`** — Returns `1_000L` with comment `"//default to nanoseconds for now"`, but `1_000L` = 1 microsecond, not 1 nanosecond. Either change the value to `1L` or correct the comment.
- [InterSystemsIRISDialect.java:491] **Pointless override of `defaultScrollMode()`** — Body is `return super.defaultScrollMode();`, which does nothing. Either return a specific `ScrollMode` or remove the override.
- [InterSystemsIRISDialect.java:754] **Consider making `datetimeFormat(String)` package-private** — Declared `public` but only called by `appendDatetimeFormat()` within the same class. Unless other dialects or users need it, reduce visibility.
- [InterSystemsIRISDialect.java:460] **Potential inconsistency between `supportsFetchClause` and `LimitHandler`** — `supportsFetchClause(ROWS_ONLY)` returns `true`, but `InterSystemsIRISLimitHandler` uses `TOP ?` for max-rows-only queries. If IRIS does not natively support `FETCH FIRST N ROWS ONLY`, set `supportsFetchClause` to `false` so Hibernate handles translation consistently.
- [InterSystemsIRISSqlAstTranslator.java:117] **`visitBinaryArithmeticExpression` uses backslash for integer division** — Emits ` \` when `isIntegerDivisionEmulationRequired()` is true. Please confirm this is valid IRIS TSQL syntax for integer division.

SUMMARY:
This PR (HHH-20250 Phase 2) extends Spanner PostgreSQL dialect support with 193 file changes: adds missing function registrations (count, overlay, chr, var_pop, stddev_pop, etc.), type descriptors for LocalDateTime/LocalTime/Time, SQL AST translator overrides for quantified subqueries and unary operations, adds new DialectFeatureChecks for test gating, skips unsupported tests via @SkipForDialect, and refactors docker_db.sh for multi-instance Spanner emulator support.

POINTS:
- [hibernate-core/src/test/java/org/hibernate/orm/test/jpa/criteria/basic/ExpressionsTest.java:283] **Replace "FIXIT" placeholder** — The skip reason `@SkipForDialect(dialectClass = SpannerPostgreSQLDialect.class, reason = "FIXIT")` on `testDateTimeOperations` is a TODO placeholder that must be replaced with an actual explanation of which date/time operations fail on Spanner PostgreSQL.

- [hibernate-community-dialects/src/main/java/org/hibernate/community/dialect/SpannerPostgreSQLDialect.java:621-640] **`timestampdiffPattern` uses `extract(epoch from ...)` without test coverage** — The method computes timestamp differences via `extract(epoch from ?3)-extract(epoch from ?2)` for sub-day units. This differs from the parent `PostgreSQLDialect.timestampdiffPattern()` which uses `extract(field from ?3-?2)` on interval subtraction. Spanner PostgreSQL's support for `extract(epoch from timestamp)` is unverified and no test exercises this method. Consider adding a test or documenting this assumption.

- [hibernate-community-dialects/src/main/java/org/hibernate/community/dialect/SpannerPostgreSQLDialect.java:883] **Add `@Override` annotation to `datetimeFormat(String)`** — This method shadows `PostgreSQLDialect.datetimeFormat(String)` but lacks `@Override`. Adding it would catch future signature mismatches at compile time.

- [hibernate-core/src/test/java/org/hibernate/orm/test/hql/PostgreSQLFunctionSelectClauseTest.java:37] [hibernate-core/src/test/java/org/hibernate/orm/test/hql/PostgreSQLFunctionWhereClauseTest.java:33] **Vague skip reasons "Spanner doesn't support functions"** — Both test classes use this generic reason. Since they register custom `FunctionContributor` functions, the reason should specify which PostgreSQL-specific functions are unsupported (e.g., "Spanner doesn't support array_agg or custom function registration").

- [hibernate-community-dialects/src/main/java/org/hibernate/community/dialect/SpannerPostgreSQLTableExporter.java:47-49] **`isSequenceTable` heuristic is fragile** — The check `table.getColumnSpan() == 1 && table.getInitCommands(context).size() == 1` is a reasonable heuristic for detecting Hibernate's table-based sequence emulator tables, but could theoretically match other single-column tables with init commands. Consider adding a comment explaining the assumption.

- [hibernate-community-dialects/src/main/java/org/hibernate/community/dialect/CommunityDatabase.java:231] **SPANNER_PG placement disrupts alphabetical ordering** — The new `SPANNER_PG` entry is inserted between GAUSSDB and DERBY. Maintaining alphabetical order (placing it between SINGLESTORE and SYBASE_ANYWHERE) would improve consistency.

- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/dialect/function/SpannerPostgreSQLTruncFunction.java:27] **`SpannerPostgreSQLTruncFunction` passes `false` to parent constructor** — The `false` parameter disables two-argument trunc support in `PostgreSQLTruncFunction`, but the PR's `render()` override in `SpannerPostgreSQLTruncRoundFunction` handles both one and two arguments. Verify this alignment is intentional.

- [hibernate-community-dialects/src/main/java/org/hibernate/community/dialect/SpannerPostgreSQLDialect.java:270] **`LimitOffsetLimitHandler` vs parent `OffsetFetchLimitHandler`** — `PostgreSQLDialect` uses `OffsetFetchLimitHandler` (LIMIT/OFFSET + FETCH FIRST). `SpannerPostgreSQLDialect` overrides with `LimitOffsetLimitHandler` (LIMIT/OFFSET only). This is correct if Spanner PostgreSQL doesn't support FETCH FIRST syntax, but worth documenting.

- [docker_db.sh] **Emulator port allocation uses sequential ports 9010+n and 9020+n** — With DB_COUNT potentially up to 8, this allocates ports 9011-9018 and 9021-9028. Verify these ports don't conflict with other CI database services and consider documenting the port range requirement.

SUMMARY:
This PR adds a new InterSystems IRIS dialect to the hibernate-community-dialects module with 5 new files covering dialect configuration, SQL AST translation, a log function, identity column support, and pagination limit handling. The implementation follows established Hibernate dialect patterns but has several issues including duplicate registrations, missing dialect discovery configuration, use of deprecated APIs, and a missing modern limit handler overload.

POINTS:
- [InterSystemsIRISDialect.java:229-234] Duplicate function factory calls: `functionFactory.nowCurdateCurtime()` is called twice (first around line 121, again at line 229); `functionFactory.yearMonthDay()` is called twice (lines 230 and 232); `functionFactory.weekQuarter()` is called twice (lines 231 and 233). These redundant registrations should be removed.

- [InterSystemsIRISDialect.java:141] Missing dialect registration for auto-discovery: Community dialects must be registered in `CommunityDatabase.java` (for JDBC metadata auto-detection) and `CommunityDialectSelector.java` (for name-based resolution). Without these entries, users cannot configure this dialect using a short name like `"intersystems-iris"` — they must use the fully-qualified class name.

- [InterSystemsIRISLimitHandler.java:26-54] Missing modern `processSql` overload for Hibernate 7.1: The implementation only overrides the deprecated `processSql(String sql, Limit limit)` method. The modern API requires `processSql(String sql, int jdbcParameterCount, ParameterMarkerStrategy, QueryOptions)` to properly support named parameters (`:param`) and non-standard parameter marker strategies. See how `OffsetFetchLimitHandler` and `TopLimitHandler` implement both overloads using `insertAfterSelect()`, `insertAfterDistinct()`, and `parameterMarkerStrategy.createMarker()`.

- [InterSystemsIRISLogFunction.java:27-50] Deprecated `render()` method signature: The overridden `render(SqlAppender, List, SqlAstTranslator)` uses the deprecated 3-parameter signature marked `@Deprecated(forRemoval = true)`. Should use the 4-parameter version `render(SqlAppender, List, ReturnableType, SqlAstTranslator)` as shown in current implementations like `PGVectorNormFunction`.

- [InterSystemsIRISDialect.java:122-126] Questionable `dateadd` function parameter types: The signature is `(STRING string, NUMERIC unit, ANY datetime)` but the conventional dateadd semantics are `dateadd(part, number, date)` — the second parameter is typically the numeric value to add, and the first is the date part (e.g., 'day', 'month'). The parameter types appear mismatched with the documented usage.

- [InterSystemsIRISDialect.java:130-134] Questionable `datediff` return type: The function declares `setInvariantType(stringType)` but datediff typically returns a numeric value (the count of intervals between two dates). Consider using `integerType` or `doubleType` as the return type.

- [InterSystemsIRISDialect.java:455] Redundant `supportsSubqueryOnMutatingTable()` method: This method is defined but is not an override of any method in `Dialect` — it may be intended as a custom getter but has no `@Override` annotation and no callers. Either add the override to the base class or remove it.

- [InterSystemsIRISDialect.java:388-403] `contributeTypes()` re-registers types already in the base registry: `TimestampJdbcType.INSTANCE` is added via `jdbcTypeRegistry.addDescriptor(...)` after `super.contributeTypes()` has already registered standard types. This may cause duplicate descriptors or unexpected behavior. Consider whether these registrations are truly necessary beyond what `registerColumnTypes()` already handles.

- [InterSystemsIRISSqlAstTranslator.java:136] Questionable column qualification logic: The `determineColumnReferenceQualifier()` method contains complex logic referencing `InsertSelectStatement` and `DmlTargetColumnQualifierSupport`. Verify this against the standard implementation in `AbstractSqlAstTranslator.determineColumnReferenceQualifier()` — the current logic appears to mirror it but may have edge case differences, particularly around the `!getQueryPartStack().isEmpty()` check.

SUMMARY:
This PR adds `array_reverse()` and `array_sort()` HQL functions with native and emulated implementations across PostgreSQL (18+ native, earlier unnest-based), Oracle (PL/SQL UDFs), H2 (system_range-based), HSQLDB (sort_array/unnest), and CockroachDB (unnest emulation). The implementation is comprehensive with proper Criteria API support, documentation, and tests.

POINTS:
- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/dialect/function/array/HSQLArraySortFunction.java:62-65] Question: Does HSQLDB's `sort_array()` function actually support `ASC/DESC NULLS FIRST/NULLS LAST` suffix clauses? The `renderWithLiteralArguments` method appends these directly (e.g., `sort_array(expr) desc nulls last)`), but if HSQLDB doesn't support this syntax, queries with literal sort parameters will fail at runtime. The `renderWithExpressionArguments` fallback uses `unnest()`+`array_agg()` which is safer, but the literal path remains unverified against HSQLDB documentation.
- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/dialect/function/array/PostgreSQLArrayReverseEmulation.java:29] The Javadoc says "HSQLDB uses the same approach" but HSQLDB's `arrayReverse_unnest()` method in CommonFunctionFactory explicitly registers `PostgreSQLArrayReverseEmulation` — so technically HSQLDB does use this class. The javadoc could be clearer: "Emulation also used by HSQLDB via `arrayReverse_unnest()`."
- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/dialect/type/OracleUserDefinedTypeExporter.java:233] The generated PL/SQL functions include the `deterministic` keyword, but the functions execute dynamic SQL via `table(arr)` and `select ... from table(arr)`. Oracle's `deterministic` hint requires truly pure functions, and dynamic SQL with `table()` casts may not qualify. Consider removing `deterministic` from the generated function bodies to avoid potential Oracle optimizer issues.
- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/dialect/function/array/PostgreSQLArraySortEmulation.java:140] The `needsArrayCasting` method returns `true` only for string element types. This follows the existing pattern in `PostgreSQLArrayTrimEmulation`, but worth confirming that no other element types (e.g., numeric arrays) need explicit casting in the `coalesce(array_agg(...), cast(array[] as TYPE))` fallback. If the element type differs between the `unnest` output and the expected return type, PostgreSQL may reject the query without explicit casting.
- [hibernate-core/src/test/java/org/hibernate/orm/test/function/array/ArraySortTest.java:105-121] `testArraySortWithParameters` tests bound parameters (`:desc`, `:nullsFirst`) with values `(true, false)`, which triggers the dynamic ORDER BY CASE WHEN path. Consider adding a test case with `(false, true)` parameters to verify ascending-with-nulls-first works correctly through the expression rendering path, not just the literal path.
- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/dialect/function/array/H2ArrayReverseFunction.java:38-44] The H2 emulation uses a hardcoded `maximumArraySize` (default 1000 from `H2Dialect.getMaximumArraySize()`) in `system_range(1, maximumArraySize)`. This is consistent with existing H2 array function emulations, but note that if an array exceeds this limit, only the first N elements will be reversed. This is documented in the existing H2 emulations and is acceptable given H2's limitations.
- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/query/sqm/internal/SqmCriteriaNodeBuilder.java:5443-5448] The `collectionSort` methods delegate to the `array_sort` function internally. This is consistent with the existing `collectionTrim` pattern. The method signatures return `JpaExpression<C>` where `C extends Collection<?>`, and the underlying SQL function produces an array result — verify that Hibernate's result mapping correctly converts array-to-Collection for `@ElementCollection` mappings. This conversion path should already exist from other collection array functions.

SUMMARY:
This PR adds Iceberg format v3 deletion vector support, enabling DELETE/UPDATE/MERGE on v3 tables. The implementation includes a new `DeletionVector` class with custom serialization, integration with the Puffin blob format for storage, merging logic for v2→v3 upgrades, and read-path filtering. The integration test `testIcebergWritesAndTrinoReadsDeletionVector` confirms cross-format compatibility with the Iceberg library.

POINTS:
- [plugin/trino-iceberg/src/main/java/io/trino/plugin/iceberg/delete/DefaultDeletionVectorWriter.java:170] Typo: "Delection vector is empty" should be "Deletion vector is empty". Same typo appears at `IcebergMetadata.java:3317` ("existing delection vectors" → "existing deletion vectors").

- [plugin/trino-iceberg/src/main/java/io/trino/plugin/iceberg/IcebergPageSourceProvider.java:495-496] The `orElseThrow()` calls on `contentOffset` and `contentSizeInBytes` have no error message. If a malformed delete file lacks these fields, debugging will be difficult. Add descriptive messages like `.orElseThrow(() -> new IllegalStateException("contentOffset missing from deletion vector delete file: " + delete.path()))`.

- [plugin/trino-iceberg/src/main/java/io/trino/plugin/iceberg/delete/DeletionVector.java:171-174] The `RoaringBitmap[] deletedRows` sparse array uses index = `pos >>> 32` (high 32 bits of file position). For data files with positions beyond `Integer.MAX_VALUE` (2^31 rows), this creates a sparse array with index up to `(maxPosition >>> 32)`. A file with row position 0x10000000000 (2^40) would create an array of size 0x1000 = 4096, which is manageable. However, the `getOrCreateBitmap` method grows the array one slot at a time with `Arrays.copyOf`. Consider using a `Map<Integer, RoaringBitmap>` for files with very large or scattered high-32-bit keys.

- [plugin/trino-iceberg/src/main/java/io/trino/plugin/iceberg/delete/DeleteManager.java:100-104] The `onlyElement()` collector assumes exactly one row position column exists. If `readColumns` somehow contains zero or multiple row position columns (shouldn't happen in practice), this throws `IllegalArgumentException`. Consider a more defensive approach like finding the first matching column or documenting the invariant.

- [plugin/trino-iceberg/src/main/java/io/trino/plugin/iceberg/delete/DeleteManager.java:85-89] The `.or()` fallback to `PositionDeleteReader.readPositionDeletes` is evaluated lazily, so it correctly provides a fallback when the DV is absent. However, if the DV file is present but corrupted/unreadable (e.g., IOException), the exception propagates and position delete files are NOT used as fallback. This is a correctness concern: corrupted DVs cause data loss by skipping position deletes. Consider catching the exception and falling back.

- [plugin/trino-iceberg/src/main/java/io/trino/plugin/iceberg/delete/DeletionVector.java:80] The comment "the deletion vector randomly mixes big-endian and little-endian integers" accurately describes the Iceberg DV spec. The integration test `testIcebergWritesAndTrinoReadsDeletionVector` confirms cross-format compatibility. However, the `crc32()` method calls `data.byteArray()` which throws `UnsupportedOperationException` for direct slices. While the current callers use heap-backed slices (Slices.allocate/Slices.wrappedBuffer), consider adding a verification or switching to `data.getBytes()` for defensive coding.

- [plugin/trino-iceberg/src/main/java/io/trino/plugin/iceberg/delete/PositionDeleteReader.java:136] TODO comment: "add specialized code for RLE and dictionary blocks" — this optimization should be tracked as a follow-up, since path column values in position delete files are typically dictionary-encoded.

- [plugin/trino-iceberg/src/main/java/io/trino/plugin/iceberg/IcebergMergeSink.java:145-166] For v3, the merge sink creates CommitTaskData with empty path ("") and zero size, carrying the serialized deletion vector in `serializedDeletionVector`. This is a clean design — the actual Puffin file is written later by `DefaultDeletionVectorWriter.writeDeletionVectorsPuffin`.

- [plugin/trino-iceberg/src/main/java/io/trino/plugin/iceberg/IcebergSplitSource.java:737-745] The new `verifyDeletionVectorReferencesDataFile` validation correctly checks that DV metadata (contentOffset, contentSizeInBytes, referencedDataFile) matches the scan task's data file. This replaces the previous blanket rejection of PUFFIN format with targeted validation.

- [plugin/trino-iceberg/src/test/java/io/trino/plugin/iceberg/TestIcebergV3.java:836-887] The v2→v3 migration test (`testV2ToV3MigrationWithDeletes`) provides good coverage of the upgrade scenario, verifying that existing position delete files remain effective after upgrade and new deletes use deletion vectors.

SUMMARY:
This PR adds an optional in-memory Parquet footer cache for the Iceberg connector to reduce tail-read latency on object stores. The design is clean and well-scoped: the parquet library provides the cache interface, Iceberg owns configuration and file identity (location + size), and all existing callers (Hive, Delta, Hudi, Redshift) are unaffected since the new 6-parameter `readFooter` overload won't collide with existing 2/4/5-param signatures.

POINTS:
- [lib/trino-parquet/src/main/java/io/trino/parquet/cache/MemoryParquetFooterCache.java:54] The comment says "Do not replace an existing value, and only copy footer bytes when the cache is missing this key" but `cache.get(key, footerBytes::copy)` WILL replace an existing value with the newly computed one if the key already exists. If the intent is truly "only insert when absent", use `cache.putIfAbsent(key, footerBytes.copy())` instead. Current behavior is not a correctness bug (footers are identical), but the comment is misleading and concurrent puts for the same key waste the `copy()` allocation.

- [lib/trino-parquet/src/main/java/io/trino/parquet/cache/ParquetFooterCache.java:23] The `get` method declares `throws IOException` but neither implementation (`MemoryParquetFooterCache` nor `NoOpParquetFooterCache`) throws a checked IOException. Callers in MetadataReader catch `IOException | RuntimeException` around cache.get() calls. Either remove `throws IOException` from the interface or document when it would be thrown.

- [lib/trino-parquet/src/main/java/io/trino/parquet/cache/NoOpParquetFooterCache.java:18-22] The class is declared as `enum` (implicitly final) which means it cannot override `invalidate` even though it explicitly declares it. Since the interface provides a default empty implementation, this works today, but if a future cache implementation needs non-trivial invalidate behavior, the enum pattern would be problematic. Consider making this a regular class with private constructor.

- [lib/trino-parquet/src/main/java/io/trino/parquet/reader/MetadataReader.java:180] Method `readFooterBytes` returns `completeFooterSize` bytes (metadata + post-script), but the cached value includes the magic number and length prefix which are redundant. `parseFooter` then slices out just the metadata portion. This is correct but inflates cached data. Consider caching only `buffer.slice(buffer.length() - completeFooterSize, metadataLength)` to save ~8 bytes per entry (minor).

- [lib/trino-parquet/src/main/java/io/trino/parquet/cache/MemoryParquetFooterCache.java:46] The guard `footerBytes.length() > maxSizeInBytes` is redundant because the weigher already prevents entries exceeding `maxSizeInBytes` from being retained. However, keeping it is a reasonable fast-path optimization to avoid the cache operation entirely for oversized footers.

- [plugin/trino-iceberg/src/main/java/io/trino/plugin/iceberg/IcebergPageSourceProvider.java:1023] The cache key uses `inputFile.location().toString()` as the file identity. Investigation confirms `InputFile.location()` returns a `Location` whose `toString()` returns the original, unmodified path string from Iceberg metadata — deterministic and stable across reads. This is correct.

- [lib/trino-parquet/src/main/java/io/trino/parquet/reader/MetadataReader.java:93-115] The new cached `readFooter` overload catches `IOException | RuntimeException` from cache.get() and calls `invalidateParquetFooterCache`, but `invalidateParquetFooterCache` itself catches RuntimeException and silently swallows it. If `cache.get()` throws IOException (even though no current implementation does), the invalidation call would also be wrapped in a try-catch that could mask the IOException. This is defensive but worth noting.

- [plugin/trino-iceberg/src/test/java/io/trino/plugin/iceberg/TestIcebergPageSourceProvider.java:196-274] Good test coverage for cache miss/hit and corrupt cache fallback. Consider adding a test for encrypted footers (`EMAGIC`) since `parseFooter` handles them differently (decryption context, footer signer verification). The cache stores raw bytes before decryption, so it should work, but explicit test coverage would increase confidence.

- [lib/trino-parquet/pom.xml:53-57] Adding `trino-cache` dependency is correct. Investigation confirms `EvictableCacheBuilder.maximumWeight(long)` and `.weigher()` are valid APIs delegating to Guava's `CacheBuilder`, and the returned cache is thread-safe via ConcurrentHashMap + synchronized blocks.

- [lib/trino-parquet/src/main/java/io/trino/parquet/reader/MetadataReader.java:188-190] The `invalidateParquetFooterCache` helper is private and only called within MetadataReader — good encapsulation. The method silently swallows RuntimeException to avoid cache failures affecting reads, which is the right design for an optional optimization.

SUMMARY:
This PR adds failure analysis for missing web server factory beans by introducing a new exception type and failure analyzer. The analyzer has a critical design flaw in its type parameter that causes it to always return null when the target exception is top-level, and it's registered against a broad exception type causing unnecessary invocations for every ApplicationContextException.

POINTS:
- [spring-boot-project/spring-boot/src/main/java/org/springframework/boot/web/server/context/MissingWebServerFactoryBeanFailureAnalyzer.java:30] **Critical: Analyzer extends wrong generic type.** The analyzer extends `AbstractFailureAnalyzer<ApplicationContextException>` but then checks `cause.getCause() instanceof MissingWebServerFactoryBeanException`. Since `MissingWebServerFactoryBeanException extends ApplicationContextException`, the inherited `findCause()` method will match the `MissingWebServerFactoryBeanException` directly and pass it as the `cause` parameter — making `cause.getCause()` return `null`. The analyzer will **always return null** when the exception is the top-level one. Fix: Extend `AbstractFailureAnalyzer<MissingWebServerFactoryBeanException>` directly (following the pattern of `PortInUseFailureAnalyzer`) and remove the `getCause()` unwrapping logic.

- [spring-boot-project/spring-boot/src/main/java/org/springframework/boot/web/server/context/MissingWebServerFactoryBeanFailureAnalyzer.java:30] **Inefficiency: Broad exception type causes unnecessary invocations.** By extending `AbstractFailureAnalyzer<ApplicationContextException>`, this analyzer will be invoked for every `ApplicationContextException` in the application — including unrelated failures like port-in-use errors, bean creation failures, etc. Extending `AbstractFailureAnalyzer<MissingWebServerFactoryBeanException>` instead would limit invocations to only relevant cases.

- [spring-boot-project/spring-boot/src/main/java/org/springframework/boot/web/server/context/MissingWebServerFactoryBeanFailureAnalyzer.java:40-41] **Fragile cause chain assumption.** The `cause.getCause()` unwrapping assumes the exception is always wrapped by another `ApplicationContextException` (as done in `onRefresh()`). This is fragile — if the exception is thrown from a different code path without wrapping, or if the wrapping behavior changes, the analyzer silently fails to produce any analysis.

- [spring-boot-project/spring-boot/src/main/java/org/springframework/boot/web/server/context/MissingWebServerFactoryBeanFailureAnalyzer.java:36] **Failure message could be more actionable.** The message "the dependent class is missing" doesn't tell users which dependency to add. Consider suggesting specific starters: for SERVLET type, mention `spring-boot-starter-web` or `spring-boot-starter-tomcat`; for REACTIVE type, mention `spring-boot-starter-webflux` or `spring-boot-starter-netty`.

- [spring-boot-project/spring-boot/src/test/java/org/springframework/boot/web/server/context/MissingWebServerFactoryBeanFailureAnalyzerTests.java:54-62] **Test coverage: Missing integration test.** The tests verify the analyzer in isolation but don't test it through `SpringApplication.run()` or the `FailureAnalyzers` infrastructure. Consider adding an integration test (similar to patterns in other analyzer tests) that starts a context without a web server factory and verifies the failure analysis is produced end-to-end.

- [spring-boot-project/spring-boot/src/main/java/org/springframework/boot/web/reactive/context/ReactiveWebServerApplicationContext.java:107-109] **Minor: Exception message improvement.** The original message said "ReactiveWebApplicationContext" but the fix uses `webApplicationContextClass.getSimpleName()`, which produces "ReactiveWebServerApplicationContext". This is more accurate and the test expectation was updated accordingly.

- [src/checkstyle/import-control.xml:85-87] **Note: Import control change is correct but targets different concern.** The added rule allows the new `web.server.context` package to import from `org.springframework.context`. The cross-package import from `web.reactive.context` to `web.server.context` is already permitted by the existing `<allow pkg="org.springframework.boot.web.server" />` rule, so no additional rules are needed there.

SUMMARY:
PR #51209 introduces a new `ValueRegistry` API for storing runtime-only values that differ from configured values. The API is placed in a separate `quarkus-registry` module (parent-first) to avoid classloader issues. The implementation spans deployment-time bytecode generation, runtime Application bootstrap, Arc CDI integration, and test framework support. Overall well-structured design, but several issues need attention before merging.

POINTS:
- [test-framework/junit/src/main/java/io/quarkus/test/junit/QuarkusTestExtensionState.java:26-62] **ValueRegistry not propagated during clone()**: The `clone()` method uses reflection to extract 4 fields (`testResourceManager`, `resource`, `shutdownHook`, `clearCallbacks`) but does NOT handle the new `valueRegistry` field. When state is cloned across classloaders (which happens when created by one CL but accessed by another), the cloned instance will have `valueRegistry = null`. The TODO comment acknowledges this but no fix is provided. Add a `getValueRegistry()` getter call in `clone()` and pass it to the new constructor.

- [test-framework/junit/src/main/java/io/quarkus/test/junit/QuarkusIntegrationTestExtension.java:315] **@QuarkusIntegrationTest creates local empty ValueRegistry**: `new ValueRegistryImpl.Builder().addDiscoveredInfos().build()` creates a fresh registry populated only by `ServiceLoader<RuntimeInfoProvider>`. Since integration tests run the app in a separate JVM via `ArtifactLauncher`, this local registry has NO runtime values (HTTP port, etc.). In contrast, `@QuarkusTest` retrieves the actual app's registry via `runningQuarkusApplication.valueRegistry()`. This semantic gap means integration tests cannot verify actual runtime values. Consider documenting this limitation or providing a mechanism (e.g., via system properties) for the remote JVM to report values back.

- [core/runtime/src/main/java/io/quarkus/runtime/ValueRegistryConfigSource.java:44-48] **Potential NPE in getValue when RuntimeInfo.get() returns null**: The code `valueRegistry.get(propertyName)` returns `RuntimeInfo<?>`, then `value.get(valueRegistry).toString()` is called. If a `RuntimeInfo` implementation's `get()` method returns `null`, this throws NPE. Add a null check: `return value != null ? value.get(valueRegistry) != null ? value.get(valueRegistry).toString() : null : null`. Also, the TODO about config string conversion should be addressed or deferred to a follow-up.

- [independent-projects/bootstrap/registry/src/main/java/io/quarkus/registry/ValueRegistry.java:115] **RuntimeKey.key(Class<T>) uses Class.getName() which can produce collisions**: `RuntimeKey.key(TestConfig.class)` produces key `"io.quarkus.runtime.config.TestConfig"`. If two extensions define a class with the same fully qualified name (even different packages in different JARs using same name), this causes silent overwrites or retrieval errors. Consider using a namespaced scheme like `RuntimeKey.key("module-name", "config-name")` or requiring explicit string keys. The `intKey` factory also has the same issue.

- [core/deployment/src/main/java/io/quarkus/deployment/steps/ValueRegistryProcessor.java:10] **ServiceProviderBuildItem uses wrong class**: `ServiceProviderBuildItem.allProvidersFromClassPath(Runtime.class.getName())` references `java.lang.Runtime`, which is incorrect. This should be `RuntimeInfoProvider.class.getName()` to register the SPI providers for reflection in native image.

- [independent-projects/bootstrap/registry/src/main/java/io/quarkus/registry/ValueRegistry.java:120] **@Deprecated get(String) is on the public interface**: This internal bridge method for `ValueRegistryConfigSource` is marked `@Deprecated(forRemoval = true)` but lives on the public API. It allocates to avoid `RuntimeKey` creation, but the javadoc says "To avoid allocating" — consider making this package-private if the config source is in the same module, or document that extensions should not use it.

- [test-framework/junit/src/main/java/io/quarkus/test/junit/ValueRegistryParameterResolver.java:33] **supportsParameter may return true even when ValueRegistry lacks the key**: `supportsParameter` checks `valueRegistry.containsKey(key(parameterContext.getParameter().getType()))`, but if the registry is newly created (integration test case with local empty registry), this returns false correctly. However, the ordering in `QuarkusTestExtension.supportsParameter` (line 1095-1099) checks the ValueRegistry resolver FIRST before existing method invokers. If a test has a parameter that could be handled by either, this changes resolution priority. Verify this is intentional and doesn't break existing extension test parameter injection.

- [core/runtime/src/main/java/io/quarkus/runtime/ValueRegistryImpl.java:66] **ServiceLoader.load() uses TCCL — may miss providers in different classloader context**: During `Application` construction, the TCCL may not yet be the runtime classloader, especially in dev mode or when Application is instantiated during class initialization. Verify that `ServiceLoader.load(RuntimeInfoProvider.class)` at this point can discover all providers. In native image, this should be fine with `ServiceProviderBuildItem`, but JVM dev mode may require explicit classloader: `ServiceLoader.load(RuntimeInfoProvider.class, Thread.currentThread().getContextClassLoader())`.

- [core/deployment/src/main/java/io/quarkus/deployment/ExtensionLoader.java:230] **Confirmed safe: ObjectLoader.load always receives MethodCreator with StartupContext as param 0**: Investigation confirms all recorded bytecode (both static-init and runtime-init phases) uses `getMethodCreator("deploy", void.class, StartupContext.class)`. The inline comment questioning this can be removed or replaced with a confirmation.

- [core/runtime/src/main/java/io/quarkus/runtime/Application.java:57] **ValueRegistry constructed early in Application constructor**: `new ValueRegistryImpl.Builder().addDiscoveredInfos().build()` runs in the Application constructor before `doStart(args)`. At this point, no recorders have executed, so `ServiceLoader`-discovered `RuntimeInfoProvider` is the only population mechanism. This is intentional per design but means extensions registering values via recorders must do so AFTER this point — which they can, since the registry is stored in `StartupContext` before recorders run (via `MainClassBuildStep`).

SUMMARY:
This PR fixes Spanner-specific issues across the dialect, table exporter, SQL AST translator, and numerous test files. Key changes include: switching function registrations to proper implementations (CountFunction, ArrayAggFunction, ArrayToStringFunction), adding array type resolution for Spanner's `ARRAY<T>` syntax, custom UPDATE rendering with table aliasing, and extensive test adjustments with `@SkipForDialect` annotations and `where 1=1` workarounds for native SQL requiring WHERE clauses.

POINTS:
- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/dialect/SpannerDialect.java:350-356] The `count` function registration uses `new CountFunction(..., "||", "string", false)` with concatenation operator `"||"` and cast type `"string"`. Verify this is intentional — CountFunction's purpose is to handle `COUNT(DISTINCT ...)` emulations for types lacking native DISTINCT support. For Spanner, this means `COUNT(DISTINCT string_col)` will render as `COUNT(DISTINCT(string_col))` without extra wrapping. Confirm this matches Spanner's actual COUNT DISTINCT behavior.

- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/dialect/SpannerDialect.java:183-204] The `resolveSqlTypeDescriptor` override extracts the component type from `ARRAY<INT64>` by finding `<` and `>`, then special-cases `STRING` to `VARCHAR`. Consider: if the component type name is unrecognized, `resolveSqlTypeCode` returns null and the method falls through to `super.resolveSqlTypeDescriptor()`, which may not handle the `ARRAY<...>` syntax. This could silently produce incorrect type resolution for unsupported component types like `JSON`.

- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/dialect/sql/ast/SpannerSqlAstTranslator.java:250-260] The `visitSpannerTableUpdate` method hardcodes the alias suffix as `"1_0"` (`stem + "1_0"`). This matches Hibernate's standard alias generation convention, but if there are multiple mutation targets in a single statement (e.g., with joins), this fixed suffix could collide. Verify Spanner never encounters such scenarios, or use a dynamic counter like `registerAffectedTable` does.

- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/dialect/sql/ast/SpannerSqlAstTranslator.java:103-117] The `renderComparison` override for `ModifiedSubQueryExpression` handles `ALL`, `ANY`, and `SOME` modifiers. However, the condition `if (operator != ComparisonOperator.NOT_EQUAL && operator != ComparisonOperator.NOT_DISTINCT_FROM)` means NOT_EQUAL and NOT_DISTINCT_FROM comparisons with quantified subqueries fall through to `renderComparisonEmulateIntersect`, which may not handle the `ALL`/`ANY` semantics correctly. Consider whether NOT_EQUAL with `ALL` should be emulated (equivalent to `<> ALL(...)` meaning "not equal to any").

- [hibernate-core/src/test/java/org/hibernate/orm/test/annotations/query/Dictionary.java:36] Changed `disc as "type"` to `disc as type` to remove double quotes for Spanner compatibility. While "type" is not an ANSI reserved word, it IS reserved in SQL Server and HANA. The `testDiscriminator` test exercising this query has no `@SkipForDialect` for those databases. If this breaks SQL Server or HANA, add a targeted skip or use unreserved alias like `t_type`.

- [hibernate-core/src/test/java/org/hibernate/orm/test/bytecode/enhancement/lazy/proxy/LoadANonExistingNotFoundBatchEntityTest.java:145] Multiple test files add `where 1=1` to native SQL UPDATE/DELETE statements (e.g., `update Employee set employer_id = id where 1=1`). This is correct for Spanner's requirement that UPDATE/DELETE must have a WHERE clause. However, the native query `session.createNativeQuery("update Employee set employer_id = id where 1=1")` updates ALL rows — verify this matches the original intent of these tests (which previously had no WHERE and also affected all rows, so semantically equivalent).

- [hibernate-core/src/test/java/org/hibernate/orm/test/jpa/query/NativeQueryResultTypeAutoDiscoveryTest.java:121-241] Many `@SkipForDialect(SpannerDialect.class, reason = "Spanner maps integer types to bigint")` annotations are added. These are valid because Spanner only has INT64 (bigint), but consider whether the tests could be adapted to assert `Long` values instead of skipping entirely, improving test coverage for Spanner.

- [hibernate-testing/src/main/java/org/hibernate/testing/orm/junit/DialectFeatureChecks.java] The new `SupportsTimestampWithDateComparison` feature is well-placed adjacent to the existing `SupportsTimestampComparison`. It extends the exclusion to both `SpannerDialect` and `SpannerPostgreSQLDialect`. The existing `SupportsTimestampComparison` should remain unchanged for dialects that only need `SpannerPostgreSQLDialect` exclusion (backward compatibility for other tests).

- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/dialect/SpannerDialect.java:1091-1125] The `getQuerySequencesString()` method queries `INFORMATION_SCHEMA.SEQUENCES` with LEFT OUTER JOINs to `SEQUENCE_OPTIONS`. The `safe_cast(...)` calls and `coalesce(...)` pattern are good defensive measures for missing option values. One concern: the query hardcodes `1 as increment` — verify this matches Spanner's sequence behavior since Spanner sequences use `start_with_counter` and `skip_range` rather than traditional increment values.

SUMMARY:
This PR adds support for customizing the Victools SchemaGenerator via a CDI-based `SchemaGeneratorConfigCustomizer` interface, with built-in customizers for Jackson, Jakarta Validation, and Swagger2 modules. The approach of auto-detecting optional dependencies at build time and registering corresponding customizer beans is clean and follows Quarkus conventions. Key concerns include a missing ordering convention in the new interface, potential native-image reflection gaps, and documentation formatting issues.

POINTS:
- [core/runtime/src/main/java/io/quarkiverse/mcp/server/runtime/SchemaGeneratorConfigCustomizer.java:1-20] The new `SchemaGeneratorConfigCustomizer` interface is missing documentation about priority-based ordering. All other similar `@All List<...>` interfaces in this codebase (`ToolFilter`, `PromptFilter`, `ResourceFilter`, `ResourceTemplateFilter`, `InitialCheck`) document that "Multiple [items] are sorted by `InjectableBean#getPriority()` and executed sequentially. Higher priority is executed first." Since customizers modify the same `SchemaGeneratorConfigBuilder` instance and execution order matters when multiple customizers configure the same options, add this documentation for consistency and to inform users who write custom customizers.

- [core/runtime/src/main/java/io/quarkiverse/mcp/server/runtime/SchemaGeneratorConfigCustomizerJackson.java:44] The `getConfiguredOptions()` method enables several options by default (`respectJsonPropertyOrder`, `respectJsonPropertyRequired`, `flattenedEnumsFromJsonValue` are all `@WithDefault("true")`). Consider whether these defaults are appropriate for all users — enabling `RESPECT_JSONPROPERTY_REQUIRED` and `RESPECT_JSONPROPERTY_ORDER` by default changes schema generation behavior compared to the previous baseline (no Jackson module). This is a behavioral change for users who add the Jackson dependency but may not expect these options enabled.

- [core/deployment/src/main/java/io/quarkiverse/mcp/server/deployment/JsonSchemaGeneratorProcessor.java:34-43] The `JsonSchemaGeneratorProcessor` does not register the victools module classes (`JacksonModule`, `JakartaValidationModule`, `Swagger2Module`, and their corresponding `*Option` enums) for reflection. These classes are instantiated via `new JacksonModule(options)`, etc. at runtime in the customizer classes. While the base code's use of `SchemaGenerator` and `SchemaGeneratorConfigBuilder` also lacks reflection registration (a pre-existing concern), this PR extends the surface area. Consider whether `ReflectiveClassBuildItem` registration is needed for native-image compatibility when these optional modules are present.

- [docs/modules/ROOT/pages/index.adoc:568-569] Two documentation issues: (1) "the schema generator server will be automatically configured" contains an extra word — should be "the schema generator will be automatically configured". (2) Missing closing backtick: the text reads `` `com.github.victools:jsonschema-module-jakarta-validation and `com.github.victools:jsonschema-module-swagger-2` `` but should be `` `com.github.victools:jsonschema-module-jakarta-validation` and `com.github.victools:jsonschema-module-swagger-2` ``.

- [transports/sse/deployment/src/test/java/io/quarkiverse/mcp/server/test/McpServerTest.java:46-48] Good practice: the base test class disables all three schema generator modules by default in `defaultConfig()`, ensuring existing tests are not affected by the new optional dependencies added to the test classpath. This prevents test flakiness and maintains backward compatibility in the test suite.

- [core/runtime/src/main/java/io/quarkiverse/mcp/server/runtime/ToolManagerImpl.java:244-252] The `constructSchemaGenerator` method applies customizers in CDI injection order (via `@All List<...>`). While CDI respects `@Priority` for ordering, there's no explicit `Prioritized` extension on the interface. This is consistent with the existing `ToolFilter` pattern in the codebase, but the lack of documented ordering contract (see point 1) makes it unclear whether users should rely on priority annotations to control customizer sequencing.

SUMMARY:
This PR adds support for subqueries referencing the target table in MariaDB DML (pre-11.1) by switching to single-table syntax when target table correlation is detected. Three new AST walker classes (CorrelationChecker, NestedCorrelationChecker, NestedOrTargetTableCorrelationVisitor) detect correlation patterns, and both MariaDB translators are updated to emit single-table DML and skip subquery wrappers when correlation is present. The core logic is sound and well-tested.

POINTS:
- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/sql/ast/spi/AbstractSqlAstTranslator.java:6181-6183] `hasCorrelatedSubquery(Statement, String)` is defined but never called anywhere in the codebase (confirmed via grep). It's the only caller of `CorrelationChecker.hasCorrelation(SqlAstNode, String)`. If this is intentional scaffolding for future use, add a `@see` or comment; otherwise consider removing to avoid maintenance burden.

- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/sql/ast/spi/NestedOrTargetTableCorrelationVisitor.java:59-61] The guard `currentLevelAliases != null` implicitly means "we are inside a subquery context." This works correctly (traversal starts from a predicate, so the first `visitQuerySpec` always enters a subquery), but the intent is not self-documenting. Consider using an explicit `inSubquery` boolean flag (as `CorrelationChecker` does) or add a clarifying comment explaining why `currentLevelAliases != null` serves as a subquery guard.

- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/dialect/sql/ast/MariaDBSqlAstTranslator.java:117-163, hibernate-community-dialects/src/main/java/org/hibernate/community/dialect/MariaDBLegacySqlAstTranslator.java:103-161] Both MariaDB translators have nearly identical implementations of `renderDeleteClause`, `renderUpdateClause`, `renderDmlTargetTableExpression`, `determineColumnReferenceQualifier`, `usesSingleTableDml`, and `needsDmlSubqueryWrapper`. Since both extend `SqlAstTranslatorWithOnDuplicateKeyUpdate<T>`, consider extracting the shared single-table DML logic into the common superclass or a dedicated mixin class to reduce duplication.

- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/sql/ast/spi/CorrelationChecker.java:62-67] The `inSubquery` boolean flag is always `true` when `hasCorrelation(MutationStatement, String)` detects a column reference (because traversal starts from predicates/expressions, not QuerySpecs). The flag matters only for the `hasCorrelation(SqlAstNode, String)` overload, which is currently unused. Consider documenting this distinction or consolidating the logic if the SqlAstNode overload is intended for general reuse.

- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/sql/ast/tree/Statement.java:14] Making `Statement extends SqlAstNode` is structurally safe (Statement already declares `accept(SqlAstWalker walker)` with identical signature), but verify that no code relies on `instanceof SqlAstNode` exclusion patterns — i.e., code that checks `!(node instanceof SqlAstNode)` to filter out non-tree nodes.

SUMMARY:
This PR adds TLS client authentication (RFC 8705) support to AuthenticationOAuth2 via a new TlsClientAuthFlow class, introducing SSL context management with optional auto-refresh, a new TokenEndpointAuthMethod enum, and proper token request body construction. Key concerns include an AsyncHttpClient double-close bug (pre-existing in ClientCredentialsFlow, inherited by TlsClientAuthFlow), logging annotation inconsistency, unused parameters passed to ClientCredentialsFlow, and an untested hybrid enum parsing pattern.

POINTS:
- [pulsar-client/src/main/java/org/apache/pulsar/client/impl/auth/oauth2/FlowBase.java:227-231] **Double-close of AsyncHttpClient**: `close()` calls `httpClient.close()`, but subclasses (`ClientCredentialsFlow.close()` at line 181, `TlsClientAuthFlow.close()` at line 154) also call `exchanger.close()` which invokes `TokenClient.close()` (TokenClient.java:46) that closes the **same** httpClient reference. The AsyncHttpClient is closed twice — first by FlowBase.close(), second by TokenClient.close(). This is a pre-existing bug in ClientCredentialsFlow inherited by the new TlsClientAuthFlow. Fix: TokenClient should not close the httpClient since it doesn't own it (it receives it as a constructor parameter).

- [pulsar-client/src/main/java/org/apache/pulsar/client/impl/auth/oauth2/TlsClientAuthFlow.java:41] **Logging inconsistency**: Uses `@Slf4j` (`import lombok.extern.slf4j.Slf4j;`) while all other files in the `auth.oauth2` package use `@CustomLog` (`lombok.CustomLog`) which maps to structured slog logging per lombok.config. FlowBase, ClientCredentialsFlow, and AuthenticationOAuth2 all use `@CustomLog`. Should use `@CustomLog` for consistency.

- [pulsar-client/src/main/java/org/apache/pulsar/client/impl/auth/oauth2/AuthenticationFactoryOAuth2.java:314-317] **Dead parameters forwarded to ClientCredentialsFlow**: When `tokenEndpointAuthMethod` is `CLIENT_SECRET_POST`, the builder still passes `certFile(tlsCertFile)`, `keyFile(tlsKeyFile)`, and `autoCertRefreshDuration(autoCertRefreshDuration)` to ClientCredentialsFlow. These are never used in ClientCredentialsFlow — they default to null, are accepted by the FlowBase constructor, but have no effect (FlowBase skips the TLS cert branch when both are blank, and `scheduleSslContextRefreshIfEnabled` returns early when `sslFactory == null`). This is harmless but misleading; consider not passing these params to ClientCredentialsFlow to avoid confusion.

- [pulsar-client/src/main/java/org/apache/pulsar/client/impl/auth/oauth2/protocol/TokenEndpointAuthMethod.java:35-38] **fromValue() uses hybrid pattern with no tests**: The `equalsIgnoreCase()` matching + `IllegalArgumentException` throwing is a hybrid pattern not seen elsewhere (codebase uses either case-insensitive+null or case-sensitive+exception). No tests verify case-insensitivity (e.g., `fromValue("TLS_CLIENT_AUTH")`) or that invalid input throws. Consider adding unit tests or aligning with the existing `OffloadedReadPriority.fromString()` convention (case-sensitive + exception).

- [pulsar-client/src/main/java/org/apache/pulsar/client/impl/auth/oauth2/FlowBase.java:225-227] **Minor: potential race between scheduler shutdown and sslFactory.close()**: `shutdownNow()` does not guarantee in-flight `refreshSslContext()` tasks complete before `sslFactory.close()` is called. If `refreshSslContext()` is mid-execution (calling `sslFactory.update()`), `sslFactory.close()` could race. This mirrors the existing pattern in `HttpClient.java:171-176`, so it's consistent with codebase conventions, but worth noting.

- [pulsar-client/src/main/java/org/apache/pulsar/client/impl/auth/oauth2/FlowBase.java:110-129] **Question: certFile/keyFile validation triggers SSL factory init before Flow is actually used**: `defaultHttpClient()` initializes `PulsarSslFactory` eagerly in the constructor. For lazy-initialized flows (TlsClientAuthFlow lazily calls `initialize()` only on first `authenticate()`), this means SSL resources are allocated at construction time rather than on first use. Is eager initialization intentional, or should SSL setup be deferred to `initialize()`?

SUMMARY:
This PR introduces `compression_codec` as an Iceberg table property, replacing the session-level `iceberg.compression_codec` property. The implementation supports CREATE TABLE, ALTER TABLE SET PROPERTIES, and integrates with the writer factory. Key concerns include metadata read-time validation that could break tables created by other tools, documentation gaps for updatable properties, inconsistent case handling for stored compression values, and asymmetric treatment of Parquet vs ORC/AVRO defaults.

POINTS:
- [docs/src/main/sphinx/connector/iceberg.md:~line 970] The ALTER TABLE SET PROPERTIES documentation lists updatable properties (format, format_version, partitioning, sorted_by, max_commit_retry, object_store_layout_enabled, data_location) but does not include `compression_codec`. Since this PR enables `ALTER TABLE ... SET PROPERTIES compression_codec = 'GZIP'`, the docs should list `compression_codec` as an updatable table property.

- [plugin/trino-iceberg/src/main/java/io/trino/plugin/iceberg/IcebergUtil.java:getIcebergTableProperties()] `validateCompression(fileFormat, compressionCodec)` is invoked during metadata reads via `getTableMetadata()` (IcebergMetadata.java:949). If an existing table has an incompatible compression codec (e.g., LZ4 on Parquet/Avro created by Spark or another Iceberg engine), this throws `NOT_SUPPORTED` during DESCRIBE TABLE, SHOW CREATE TABLE, or any metadata query. The Hive connector does not validate format/codec compatibility during reads. Consider deferring validation to write paths only, or catching the exception and logging a warning instead of failing metadata reads.

- [plugin/trino-iceberg/src/main/java/io/trino/plugin/iceberg/IcebergUtil.java:calculateTableCompressionProperties()] The stored compression value uses `hiveCompressionCodec.name()` (uppercase), whereas the existing `createTableProperties` stored ORC compression as `.toLowerCase(Locale.ENGLISH)`. For example, `write.orc.compression-codec` would now be "ZSTD" instead of "zstd". While `getHiveCompressionCodec` handles both via `.toUpperCase(ENGLISH)`, this changes the stored representation. Verify downstream consumers (Spark, other Iceberg readers) accept uppercase codec values consistently.

- [plugin/trino-iceberg/src/main/java/io/trino/plugin/iceberg/IcebergUtil.java:createTableProperties()] When `compression_codec` is not explicitly set, the catalog-level default (`iceberg.compression-codec`, defaulting to ZSTD) is not persisted to table properties. It only serves as a runtime fallback in `IcebergFileWriterFactory`. This means `SHOW CREATE TABLE` will not display the effective compression, and the table's behavior depends on writer-side config rather than table metadata. Consider whether the catalog default should be persisted when not explicitly overridden, for consistency with how `format` defaults work.

- [plugin/trino-iceberg/src/main/java/io/trino/plugin/iceberg/IcebergUtil.java:createTableProperties()] The PR explicitly removes `write.parquet.compression-codec` when not set (to avoid Iceberg's ZSTD default per issue #20401), but does not apply the same logic to ORC/AVRO. Verify whether Iceberg also sets defaults for `write.orc.compression-codec` and `write.avro.compression-codec` when the property is absent, and apply consistent handling if so.

- [plugin/trino-iceberg/src/main/java/io/trino/plugin/iceberg/IcebergAvroFileWriter.java:~line 68] `validateCompression(AVRO, Optional.of(hiveCompressionCodec))` is called in the writer constructor, but validation already occurs earlier in `IcebergUtil.createTableProperties()` and `IcebergMetadata.setTableProperties()`. This triple-layer validation is defensive but redundant. Consider whether the writer-level check adds value given the upstream validations.

- [plugin/trino-iceberg/src/test/java/io/trino/plugin/iceberg/BaseIcebergSystemTables.java:775,790] and [plugin/trino-iceberg/src/test/java/io/trino/plugin/iceberg/TestIcebergV2.java:935] The column_size test assertions change from 45→51 and 49→52 due to different compression behavior (previously Parquet ZSTD was always set, now it may not be). Confirm these values are stable across test runs and consider adding a comment explaining the change.

- [plugin/trino-iceberg/src/main/java/io/trino/plugin/iceberg/IcebergMetadata.java:setTableProperties()] When `FILE_FORMAT_PROPERTY` is not present in the input properties, `newFileFormat` correctly retains `oldFileFormat`, and `calculateTableCompressionProperties(oldFileFormat, newFileFormat, ...)` will still process compression changes. However, the method is only called after the format check block. Verify that `calculateTableCompressionProperties` is always invoked regardless of whether format or compression is being changed, since it handles both independently.

SUMMARY:
This PR adds certificate management operations for signature verification in the Installation Manager, including import, parse, remove, and list certificates functionality. The implementation has several issues including a missing parameter registration, incomplete error handling with a `// throw exception` comment left in code, unnecessary result assignments, dead code, and a brittle test mock.

POINTS:
- [installation-manager/src/main/java/org/wildfly/core/instmgr/InstMgrCertificateImportHandler.java:81] The else branch at line 81-82 contains only a comment `// throw exception` with no actual exception thrown. When neither `cert-file` nor `certificate-content` is provided, the operation will silently succeed without importing anything. This should throw an `OperationFailedException` with a message like "One of cert-file or certificate-content must be provided."

- [installation-manager/src/main/java/org/wildfly/core/instmgr/InstMgrCertificateImportHandler.java:46] The `CERT_CONTENT` attribute is defined (line 46) but is NOT registered as a parameter in the operation definition. The `DEFINITION` at line 51 calls `addParameter(CERT_FILE)` but never `addParameter(CERT_CONTENT)`. As a result, users cannot use the `certificate-content` option via the management API — it will be silently ignored or rejected as an unknown attribute.

- [installation-manager/src/main/java/org/wildfly/core/instmgr/InstMgrUnacceptedCertificateHandler.java:83-84] Inside the for loop, `context.getResult().set(info)` is called (line 84), but this is immediately overwritten by `result.set(mCertificates)` at line 93. This intermediate assignment is unnecessary and misleading — the final `result.set(mCertificates)` at line 93 is what actually matters. Remove the `context.getResult().set(info)` line inside the loop.

- [installation-manager/src/main/java/org/wildfly/core/instmgr/logging/InstMgrLogger.java:110] The new `mutuallyExclusiveOptions` message (id=27) reads "You cannot use the '%s' option with the '%s' option because they are mutually exclusive." This is used in InstMgrCertificateImportHandler when BOTH options are provided (correct usage), but the handler also needs a DIFFERENT error for when NEITHER is provided (the incomplete `// throw exception` at line 81). Consider adding a separate message like "One of '%s' or '%s' must be provided."

- [installation-manager/src/main/java/org/wildfly/core/instmgr/cli/AddCertificatesCommand.java:75] Uses `commandInvocation.print("Importing a trusted certificate")` but should use `println` to add a newline. Additionally, the response from `this.executeOp(ctx, this.host)` at line 76 is not checked for success — if the import fails, the user sees "Importing a trusted certificate" followed by a confusing exception.

- [installation-manager/src/main/java/org/wildfly/core/instmgr/cli/ListCertificatesCommand.java:76-88] The `getAllChannelNames` method is defined but never called anywhere in the class. This is dead code and should be removed.

- [testsuite/shared/src/main/java/org/wildfly/test/installationmanager/TestInstallationManager.java:336-348] The `parseCertificate` test mock uses `while (reader.ready())` which is unreliable — `BufferedReader.ready()` can return false even when data is available. This pattern can silently skip lines or fail intermittently. Use the standard `while ((line = reader.readLine()) != null)` pattern instead.

- [installation-manager/src/main/java/org/wildfly/core/instmgr/cli/UpdateCommand.java:91-92] When `importPendingCertificates` returns `false` (user declines to import certificates), the method returns `CommandResult.SUCCESS`. However, the update was cancelled and the user will be confused to see a success result. Consider returning `CommandResult.SUCCESS` but printing a clearer "Update cancelled" message, or consider returning a different result.

SUMMARY:
This PR introduces a new `BlocksHash` SPI interface backed by `FlatHash` to reduce memory usage for Iceberg equality deletes. It replaces the per-row `RowPredicate` with a vectorized `PageFilter` approach, adds a `Positions` utility (justified copy of `SelectedPositions` due to module constraints), and guards the non-thread-safe `FlatHash` with a `ReadWriteLock`. Overall the design is sound; points below are minor concerns.

POINTS:
- [plugin/trino-iceberg/src/main/java/io/trino/plugin/iceberg/delete/Positions.java] The `Positions` class is a "simplified copy of SelectedPositions" — this is justified because the iceberg plugin has `trino-main` only as test scope, not compile scope. The established pattern exists (`PageSplitterUtil` in `trino-hive-formats` follows the same approach). No action needed, but the existing `PageSplitterUtil` comment style ("Copy of ...") could be adopted for consistency.

- [plugin/trino-iceberg/src/main/java/io/trino/plugin/iceberg/delete/FlatEqualityDeleteFilter.java:190] The `PageFilter.allOf` implementation chains `filterPositions` calls (not `applyFilter`), then calls `selectPositions` exactly once at the end via `PageFilter.applyFilter`. This is correct and avoids the dictionary-block issue. However, the test comment in `testComposedFiltersWithDictionaryBackedPage` ("If selectPositions is called multiple times...") is slightly misleading — the old `RowPredicate.and()` approach also called `selectPositions` only once when composed. The real value of `PageFilter.allOf` is that it composes at the position-level (accumulating retained positions) rather than the predicate-level, which is more natural for the new architecture. Consider clarifying the test comment.

- [plugin/trino-iceberg/src/main/java/io/trino/plugin/iceberg/delete/FlatEqualityDeleteFilter.java:204] `EXPECTED_SIZE = 1024` for the initial FlatHash capacity is quite small for large-scale upsert workloads (the test exercises 1M unique keys). The hash table will resize automatically, but starting at 1024 means the first resize could be expensive under load. Consider making this configurable (e.g., via a new config property like `iceberg.equality-deletes-initial-hash-size`) or at least documenting the resize cost in the code comment.

- [plugin/trino-iceberg/src/main/java/io/trino/plugin/iceberg/delete/FlatEqualityDeleteFilter.java:123-148] Thread safety is correctly implemented: `ReadWriteLock` guards all `FlatHash` access, `insertPage` acquires write lock, `filterPage` acquires read lock, and `volatile estimatedMemoryUsage` is read without lock (acceptable for monitoring). Under very high concurrent delete load (many threads inserting into the same `EqualityDeleteIndex`), the write lock could become a contention point. The PR description acknowledges this risk ("probably not a problem in practice given equality delete loading is IO bound") — this is reasonable.

- [core/trino-spi/src/main/java/io/trino/spi/connector/ConnectorContext.java:93] The new `default getBlocksHashFactory()` throws `UnsupportedOperationException()`. All three implementations (`ConnectorContextInstance`, `TestingConnectorContext`, `TestingPostgreSqlConnectorContext`) are correctly updated to override it. However, consider adding `@Unstable` annotation (consistent with `getFunctionBundleFactory()`) since this is a new SPI method that connectors may not implement yet.

- [plugin/trino-iceberg/src/main/java/io/trino/plugin/iceberg/delete/DeleteManager.java:157-167] The fallback path (`blocksHashFactory.isEmpty()`) correctly uses `EqualityDeleteFilter.builder()` for the legacy implementation. The feature flag default (`iceberg.equality-deletes-blocks-hash-enabled=true`) means existing deployments get the optimization automatically. The legacy path is well-tested via `TestIcebergLegacyEqualityDeletes`.

- [plugin/trino-iceberg/src/main/java/io/trino/plugin/iceberg/IcebergPageSourceProviderFactory.java:56-59] When `equalityDeletesBlocksHashEnabled=false`, the injected `BlocksHashFactory` is unused but still created. This is harmless (singleton per catalog) but note that `FlatHashStrategyCompiler` maintains a 1000-entry cache that won't be populated if the feature is disabled — no wasted memory in that case.

- [plugin/trino-iceberg/src/test/java/io/trino/plugin/iceberg/TestIcebergLegacyEqualityDeletes.java] This test class duplicates test cases from the existing Iceberg test suite but with `iceberg.equality-deletes-blocks-hash-enabled=false`. While this ensures parity between old and new paths, the file is ~340 lines of near-identical tests. Consider whether a parameterized test approach or a shared base class could reduce duplication when the feature flag is eventually removed (as the comment suggests).

SUMMARY:
This PR adds literal rendering support for Cloud Spanner, covering string escaping (backslash-based), binary literals (FROM_HEX), temporal literals (DATE/TIMESTAMP with no native TIME), and numeric literals (NUMERIC '...' wrapping for BigDecimal/BigInteger). The implementations are generally well-structured and consistent with the dialect's known type constraints.

POINTS:
- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/dialect/SpannerDialect.java:574-595] The `appendLiteral` method uses backslash escaping (`\'`) for single quotes instead of standard SQL doubling (`''`). While the comment states "Spanner uses backslash escaping," please confirm this is mandatory — does Spanner also accept the standard `''` syntax? If Spanner supports both, using `''` would be more portable and avoid the extra StringBuilder allocation. If backslash escaping is truly required, this is fine.

- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/dialect/SpannerDialect.java:578] Minor: the StringBuilder is initialized with `literal.length() + 2` accounting for quotes, but backslash escaping can expand characters (e.g., `\` → `\\`, `'` → `\'`, `\n` → `\\n`). For worst-case input (all backslashes), the builder could reallocate. Consider `literal.length() * 2 + 2` as the initial capacity, or just rely on StringBuilder's automatic expansion (which is fine if performance here is not critical).

- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/dialect/SpannerDialect.java:598-601] For `OffsetTime` inputs, the offset is preserved when constructing the `OffsetDateTime`, but `appendAsTimestampWithNanos` formats it with the offset included. Since Spanner's TIMESTAMP type represents an absolute point in time (no timezone awareness), an `OffsetTime` value like `12:00+05:00` will render as something like `TIMESTAMP '1970-01-01 12:00+05:00'` which Spanner interprets as the absolute UTC equivalent. This is **correct behavior** but worth documenting — users with timezone-aware `OffsetTime` values should understand the literal includes the offset for display but the stored value is the UTC point-in-time.

- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/dialect/SpannerDialect.java:672-684] In the Calendar-based `appendDateTimeLiteral` for `TemporalType.TIME`, `appendAsTimestampWithMillis` is used (line 677), whereas the TemporalAccessor version uses `appendAsTimestampWithNanos`. Since Calendar only has millisecond resolution, this is semantically correct — but the inconsistency between the two overloads (millis vs nanos) is worth noting. If Spanner's `timestamp` type supports nanosecond precision, future Calendar-based calls would silently lose sub-millisecond data.

- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/dialect/sql/ast/SpannerSqlAstTranslator.java:363-376] The `visitUnparsedNumericLiteral` override correctly wraps BigDecimal/BigInteger with `NUMERIC '...'` syntax. One observation: `BigDecimal.class.isAssignableFrom(javaTypeClass)` is used, but `javaTypeClass` is the actual class from the JavaTypeDescriptor — for BigDecimal/BigInteger, `isAssignableFrom` is equivalent to `==`. No issue, just noting.

- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/dialect/SpannerDialect.java:602-603] The `currentTime()` method delegates to `currentTimestamp()`. This is a pragmatic choice for Spanner (no native TIME), but callers expecting `CURRENT_TIME` (time-of-day only) will receive `CURRENT_TIMESTAMP()` (full timestamp). This is consistent with the dialect's column-level mapping but could surprise users querying temporal functions. Consider whether this needs documentation in the migration guide.

SUMMARY:
This PR introduces a new BOOTSTRAP configuration phase that allows extensions to produce ConfigSourceProviders during runtime init, before other recorders run. The implementation adds new build items (BootstrapConfigSetupCompleteBuildItem, RuntimeConfigSetupCompleteBuildItem, RunTimeConfigurationSourceValueBuildItem), refactors config generation into dedicated build steps, and updates ExtensionLoader to handle the new phase. The overall design is sound with proper build-time ordering via @Consume/@Produce annotations.

POINTS:
- [core/deployment/src/main/java/io/quarkus/deployment/steps/RuntimeConfigSetupBuildStep.java:30] Logger copy-paste error: `Logger.getLogger(ReflectiveHierarchyStep.class)` should be `Logger.getLogger(RuntimeConfigSetupBuildStep.class)`. This causes warning log messages (e.g., about ignored RuntimeValues) to be attributed to the wrong class, making debugging confusing.

- [core/deployment/src/main/java/io/quarkus/deployment/ExtensionLoader.java:845] Misleading error message: When a static bytecode recorder consumes BOOTSTRAP phase config, the error message says `"Bytecode recorder is static but an injected config object is declared as run time"` — but BOOTSTRAP is not "run time" from a user's perspective. Consider using `phase + " configuration cannot be consumed in a static recorder"` to match the improved error messages elsewhere in the same file (lines 355, 470, 542, 575, 747).

- [core/deployment/src/main/java/io/quarkus/deployment/configuration/RunTimeConfigurationGenerator.java:98] The `C_READ_CONFIG` method descriptor signature changes from `void.class` to `void.class, List.class` to accept the ConfigSourceProvider list. Verify that the generated bytecode correctly passes `Collections.emptyList()` when there are no bootstrap config sources — the RuntimeConfigSetupBuildStep handles this at line 55-56, which looks correct.

- [docs/src/main/asciidoc/writing-extensions.adoc] (not in PR) The ConfigPhase documentation table (around lines 936-965) documents BUILD_TIME, BUILD_AND_RUN_TIME_FIXED, and RUN_TIME but does not include BOOTSTRAP. This should be updated when this feature is released.

- [core/runtime/src/main/java/io/quarkus/runtime/configuration/ConfigUtils.java:89] The bootstrap config builder uses `configBuilder(false, false)` (runtime=false, addDiscovered=false). This is intentional since bootstrap config sources aren't registered yet. Confirm that custom converters needed for bootstrap config properties are registered through the generated code path (RunTimeConfigurationGenerator lines 502-508), not via ServiceLoader discovery — this appears correct.

SUMMARY:
This PR migrates 287 files from Plexus @Component/@Requirement annotations to JSR330 @Named/@Singleton/@Inject annotations. The migration approach is sound, but several files contain truncated @Named constant references that will cause compilation failures.

POINTS:
- [target-platform-configuration/src/main/java/org/eclipse/tycho/target/TargetArtifactRepositoryLayout.java] `@Named(T)` — no constant `T` is defined or imported. The class has `public static final String ID = "target"` but the annotation should be `@Named(TargetArtifactRepositoryLayout.ID)`. This will NOT compile.
- [target-platform-configuration/src/main/java/org/eclipse/tycho/target/TargetRepositoryConnectorFactory.java] `@Named(T)` — same issue. No `T` constant exists. Should be `@Named(TargetArtifactRepositoryLayout.ID)` to match the original `@Component(role = ArtifactRepositoryLayout.class, hint = TargetArtifactRepositoryLayout.ID)`.
- [tycho-baseline-plugin/src/main/java/org/eclipse/tycho/baseline/BundleArtifactBaselineComparator.java] `@Named(A)` — no constant `A` is defined or imported. The original had `@Component(role = ArtifactBaselineComparator.class, hint = ArtifactType.TYPE_ECLIPSE_FEATURE)`. Should be `@Named(ArtifactType.TYPE_ECLIPSE_FEATURE)` (and `ArtifactType` must be imported).
