The class has `static final String ROLE_HINT = "default"` and the original was `@Component(role = BuildTimestampProvider.class, hint = DefaultBuildTimestampProvider.ROLE_HINT)`. Should be `@Named(DefaultBuildTimestampProvider.ROLE_HINT)`.
- [sisu-osgi/sisu-osgi-connect/pom.xml] The javax.inject dependency was added here correctly — this ensures JSR330 annotations are available to the sisu-osgi-connect module. No issue.
- [p2-maven-plugin/src/main/java/org/eclipse/tycho/p2maven/DefaultProvisioningAgent.java] The migration of `@Requirement(hint = "connect")` to `@Inject @Named("connect")` is correct for the EquinoxServiceFactory lookup. No issue.
- [p2-maven-plugin/src/main/java/org/eclipse/tycho/p2maven/transport/TychoRepositoryTransport.java] The existing TODO comment `// TODO @Inject results in a list with multiple items of the same provider!` was preserved alongside the migration to `@Inject`. The author should investigate whether Sisu handles this role-based map injection differently than Plexus did, though this is a pre-existing concern and not introduced by the migration.

SUMMARY:
This PR adds JDT classpath attribute support for multi-release JAR compilation, enabling flexible source folder naming via a `release` attribute in `.classpath` entries. The implementation adds a `getMultiReleaseVersion()` default method to `SourceFolderClasspathEntry`, enhances `AbstractOsgiCompilerMojo.doCompile()` to check for classpath entries with release attributes before falling back to the legacy directory-based approach, and includes a comprehensive demo, integration test, and documentation.

POINTS:
- [tycho-compiler-plugin/src/main/java/org/eclipse/tycho/compiler/AbstractOsgiCompilerMojo.java:402] The new classpath-based approach streams `getEclipsePluginProject().getClasspathEntries()` and filters for `SourceFolderClasspathEntry` instances with a present `release` attribute. Since `OsgiTestCompilerMojo` inherits the full `doCompile()` flow unmodified, if test source folders are annotated with both `test="true"` and `release="N"` in `.classpath`, they would be compiled into the main bundle's `META-INF/versions/N/` output (not test-classes). Consider whether an explicit exclusion for `test="true"` entries is warranted here, or document this behavior.

- [tycho-its/src/test/java/org/eclipse/tycho/test/DemoTest.java:138] The new `testTychoMultiReleaseClasspathDemo()` test calls `runDemo("multi-release-jar-classpath")` which only verifies the build completes without error (`verifyErrorFreeLog()`). Unlike `testSignExecutableDemo()` in the same class (which validates JAR contents with `JarFile.getEntry()`), this test does not assert that version-specific classes actually landed in `META-INF/versions/9/` and `META-INF/versions/11/`. Consider adding assertions on the produced JAR structure to catch future regressions.

- [tycho-metadata-model/src/main/java/org/eclipse/tycho/model/classpath/SourceFolderClasspathEntry.java:34] The new `getMultiReleaseVersion()` default method is consistent with existing attribute-reading patterns in the codebase (cf. `ProjectClasspathEntry.isTest()` and `ClasspathParser.JDTClasspathContainer.isVintage()`). The `NumberFormatException` swallowing is appropriate since Eclipse JDT would reject invalid values anyway. One minor nit: the javadoc says "an empty OptionalInt if not" — consider clarifying this means "if not marked as a multi-release source folder or if the release value is unparseable."

- [src/site/site.xml:8] The new "Building Multi-Release JAR Bundles" menu item is inserted after "Structured Build Layout and Pomless Builds" but before "BND Workspace and Pomless Builds". Given that multi-release is a compilation feature, consider placing it closer to "Compiler Plugin" in the "Other Tycho Plugins" menu, or adding a cross-reference from the compiler plugin documentation.

- [tycho-compiler-plugin/src/main/java/org/eclipse/tycho/compiler/AbstractOsgiCompilerMojo.java:391] The legacy fallback path (`getMultiReleases()`) preserves backward compatibility for the existing `demo/multi-release-jar` demo, which has no `.classpath` file and relies entirely on `META-INF/versions/` directory scanning plus the `srcN` naming convention. This is correct — the `else` branch ensures existing projects continue to work unchanged.

- [demo/multi-release-jar-classpath/.classpath:15] The demo's JRE container references `JavaSE-1.8`, which is appropriate for the base compilation target. The version-specific sources (`src9`, `src11`) will use `--release 9` and `--release 11` respectively via the `currentRelease` field and `getReleaseLevel()` logic. This is a clean demo setup.

SUMMARY:
This PR applies `ModuleIdentifierUtil.MODULE_NAME_CORRECTOR` to module-name attributes across the codebase and removes now-redundant `parseCanonicalModuleIdentifier` calls in handlers. The core pattern is correct — the corrector canonicalizes values at write time (stripping `:main` suffix, preserving non-main slots), so handlers can use the raw value directly. However, some changes add the corrector to read-only attributes where it has no effect.

POINTS:
- [logging/src/main/java/org/jboss/as/logging/deployments/resources/HandlerResourceDefinition.java:33-35] The `MODULE` attribute is registered as **read-only** via `registerReadOnlyAttribute()` (line 84). Parameter correctors in the WildFly controller framework only execute during write operations (add/write-attribute), never during read. Adding `.setCorrector(MODULE_NAME_CORRECTOR)` here has **no effect**. Recommend reverting this change.
- [logging/src/main/java/org/jboss/as/logging/deployments/resources/LoggingDeploymentResources.java:121-123] Same issue — `MODULE` in `PropertiesResourceDefinition` is registered as read-only (line 144 via `registerReadOnlyAttribute()`). The corrector will never be invoked. Recommend reverting this change.
- [logging/src/main/java/org/jboss/as/logging/deployments/resources/HandlerResourceDefinition.java:7] and [logging/src/main/java/org/wildfly/extension/security/manager/DeploymentPermissionsResourceDefinition.java:21] The `import org.jboss.as.controller.ModuleIdentifierUtil;` added for the two logging deployment files is effectively dead code since the corrector is never used on read-only attributes.
- [elytron/src/main/java/org/wildfly/extension/elytron/DirContextDefinition.java:127-130] The `MODULE` attribute here has `.setAllowExpression(true)`. The corrector will run on literal values, which is correct, but be aware that if a user supplies an expression (e.g., `${some.module.name}`), the corrector will see the unresolved expression string — not the resolved module name. This is existing framework behavior and not a bug, just worth noting.
- [elytron/src/main/java/org/wildfly/extension/elytron/ClassLoadingAttributeDefinitions.java:44-45] Removing `ModuleIdentifierUtil.parseCanonicalModuleIdentifier(module)` and using `module` directly is correct because the corrector already canonicalized the value when the attribute was written. Same pattern correctly applied in DirContextDefinition.java:161, DiscoveryOptionAddHandler.java:75-76, and ModuleLoadingResourceDefinition.java:208.
- [controller/src/main/java/org/jboss/as/controller/extension/ExtensionResourceDefinition.java:35-36] Adding the corrector to the **deprecated** `module` parameter of `ADD_OP` is harmless and provides consistency, though deprecated parameters are no longer actively used. No issue here.
- [logging/src/main/java/org/jboss/as/logging/CommonAttributes.java:113-125] Changing `"module"`, `"name"`, `"properties"` to `ModelDescriptionConstants.MODULE`, `.NAME`, `.PROPERTIES` is a benign style improvement — the constant values are identical strings. No functional change.

SUMMARY:
This PR adds comprehensive `@Audited` read/write support to Hibernate ORM core, including a new `AuditLog` query API, `@RevisionEntity` support, collection auditing with semantic diff, inheritance hierarchy handling, and native HQL functions (`transactionId()`, `modificationType()`). The implementation is architecturally sound using a decorator pattern for mutation coordinators, but contains a confirmed NPE regression in the delete path when the entity instance is null.

POINTS:
- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/persister/entity/mutation/DeleteCoordinatorAudit.java:37-48] **NPE regression when entity is null**: The PR removes the null-safety checks from the base code's `resolveDeleteState()` method. When `session.delete("EntityName", id)` is called (entity parameter is null), `session.getPersistenceContextInternal().getEntry(null)` returns null, and `entityEntry.getEntityKey()` throws NPE. The old code had `if (entity == null) return null;` and `if (state != null)` guards that skipped audit row creation. Add `if (entityEntry == null) { return; }` before enqueuing.
- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/persister/entity/mutation/AbstractAuditCoordinator.java:enqueueAuditEntry] **Defensive null check**: The new `enqueueAuditEntry` helper also lacks null check on `entityEntry`. While called only from InsertCoordinatorAudit and UpdateCoordinatorAudit (where entity should be non-null), adding a null guard here prevents future regressions if the call site contract changes.
- [hibernate-core/src/test/java/org/hibernate/temporal/audit/ — coverage gap] **Missing test for delete-by-id**: All audit test files use `session.remove(session.find(...))` or `session.remove(entity)` where the entity is loaded first. No test exercises `session.delete("EntityName", id)` or `session.delete(EntityClass.class, id)` patterns. Add a test case to verify audit rows are correctly created when deleting an entity by its identifier without loading it first.
- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/boot/model/internal/AuditHelper.java:bindAuditTable] **Audit table default suffix changed from `_aud` to `_AUD`**: The constant `DEFAULT_TABLE_SUFFIX` changed from `"_aud"` to `"_AUD"` (line 57). This affects all new audit table names that don't specify an explicit name via `@Audited.Table`. This is a schema-breaking change for any existing audit tables created with the previous suffix. Consider documenting this in release notes.
- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/engine/spi/CollectionKey.java:37] **Sealed class change is clean**: `CollectionKey` is now `sealed ... permits TemporalCollectionKey`. Verified: no existing subclasses, all `new CollectionKey(...)` usages use the public 2-arg constructor, and the two affected internal usages in `StatefulPersistenceContext.java` are migrated to `session.generateCollectionKey(...)`.
- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/engine/spi/EntityKey.java:31] **Sealed class change is clean**: Same as above for `EntityKey`. No existing subclasses found. Constructor visibility change (second constructor to package-private) is safe since `TemporalEntityKey` is in the same package.
- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/audit/spi/AuditWorkQueue.java:170-186] **Merge matrix behavior for ADD+DEL cancellation**: When an entity is both added and deleted in the same transaction, the entries cancel out (`merged == null`). The comment notes "Collection entries may remain (orphaned) and the orphaned rows are unreachable by any query." This is technically correct but worth noting in release notes as potential unexpected behavior for users relying on strict audit completeness.
- [hibernate-core/src/main/java/org/hibernate/audit/internal/AuditLogImpl.java:100-108] **Transaction ID initialization timing change**: The PR removes `initializeCurrentTransactionIdentifier()` from `afterTransactionBegin()` in `AbstractSharedSessionContract`, making transaction ID generation lazy (on-demand in `getCurrentTransactionIdentifier()`). This is a behavioral change that should be documented, especially for `RevisionEntitySupplier` users who may depend on the transaction ID being available at transaction start.

SUMMARY:
PR replaces ConnectionBase's lock-based write queue with a new lock-free OutboundWriteQueue (MPSC pattern), introducing MessageWrite interface and refactoring HTTP/WS write paths to use the queue-based approach. Core thread safety of ConnectionBase is sound, but several issues need attention.

POINTS:
- [src/main/java/io/vertx/core/http/impl/Http1xServerResponse.java:482] The `// conn.responseComplete();` comment in `sendFile()` appears intentional (avoiding double-call since `conn.write()` auto-calls it for `LastHttpContent`), but the **failure path** is problematic: when `future.isSuccess()` is false, `LastHttpContent.EMPTY_LAST_CONTENT` is never written, and `responseComplete()` is commented out. This means on file-send failure, the connection's `responseInProgress` is never cleared and the channel may remain paused. The old code called `responseComplete()` unconditionally in the listener. Consider whether `responseComplete()` should still be called on failure, or the connection should be closed explicitly.

- [src/main/java/io/vertx/core/streams/impl/OutboundWriteQueue.java:227-229] `hook2()` is a `protected` method called from `submit()` which runs on **producer threads** (any thread submitting to the queue). There is no Javadoc specifying this thread context. A subclass overriding `hook2()` could assume it runs on the consumer thread (like `hook()` does) and introduce data races. The test `OutboundWriteQueueTest.testOrdering()` already demonstrates this by calling `queue.add(1)` from `hook2()` — `add()` is a consumer-thread-only method. Add explicit Javadoc: "@see submit — hook2 executes on the producer thread that triggered highWaterMark".

- [src/main/java/io/vertx/core/streams/impl/OutboundWriteQueue.java:103-105] `overflow` and `writeQueueFull` are documented as "Consumer thread only" fields, yet `submit()` (called by producer threads) invokes `hook2()` when `pending == highWaterMark`. While `hook2()` is a no-op in the base class, the `pending == highWaterMark` condition check from a producer thread reads the same state that `overflow` depends on. The invariant "winning this => overflow == null" in the `submit()` comment is correct due to the atomic increment, but the documentation could be clearer about which invariants hold for producer vs consumer threads.

- [src/main/java/io/vertx/core/net/impl/ConnectionBase.java:287-305] In `writeToChannel(MessageWrite msg)`, when called from a producer thread, `DRAIN_REQUIRED_MASK` triggers `eventLoop.execute(this::drainWriteQueue)`. If multiple producers submit rapidly, only one schedules drain (atomic `wip` increment), which is correct. However, if the event loop is busy and many submissions accumulate, the drain task may not execute promptly. Consider whether a bounded retry or immediate fallback is needed when the queue grows significantly before drain runs.

- [src/main/java/io/vertx/core/http/impl/Http1xClientConnection.java:394-403] The `writeWindow` and `writeOverflow` fields are removed from `Http1xClientConnection`, and the old per-stream byte-tracking backpressure logic in `writeBuffer()` is replaced with the connection-level `writeQueueFull()`. The old mechanism tracked actual bytes queued per stream; the new mechanism counts messages against fixed water marks (default 16 high / 8 low). This is a semantic change: a single large buffer and 16 small buffers are treated identically. Verify this aligns with the intended backpressure semantics, especially for pooled HTTP clients where large response bodies could fill the queue with few messages.

- [src/test/java/io/vertx/core/http/Http1xTest.java:5057] The test `testSendFilePipelined` reduces `n` from 4 to 2. If this is a workaround for stability issues with the new queue implementation, document why. If it's unrelated, add a comment explaining the change.

- [src/main/java/io/vertx/core/net/impl/ConnectionBase.java:113-120] The `writeQueue` consumer predicate checks `writable` (channel writability) at drain time, but `writable` is set from `handleChannelWritabilityChanged()` which is triggered by Netty's `channelWritabilityChanged`. There's a window where the queue drains while `writable` is still false (before Netty fires the event), causing messages to accumulate in `overflow`. The `handleChannelWritabilityChanged()` method does re-drain when the channel becomes writable, but consider whether this latency window could cause issues for latency-sensitive workloads.

SUMMARY:
PIP-473 proposes a sound architectural shift (metadata-store-backed transactional state) but omits several interface obligations, mismatches existing method signatures, and under-specifies the MetadataStore API extensions and per-topic selection mechanism needed to realize the design.

POINTS:
- [pip-473.md:~200-240] `MetadataTransactionBuffer` table lists ~5 methods (`appendBufferToTxn`, `commit`/`abort`, `getMaxReadPosition`, `isTxnAborted`, `recover`), but the `TransactionBuffer` interface (`pulsar-broker/src/main/java/org/apache/pulsar/broker/transaction/buffer/TransactionBuffer.java`) has **20 methods**. Critical gaps: `openTransactionBufferReader(TxnID, long)` — used by consumers to re-read buffered entries; `syncMaxReadPositionForNormalPublish(Position, boolean)` — maintains correct read horizon when normal and transactional writes interleave; `purgeTxns(List<Long>)` — managed-ledger offloading/GC integration; `getTransactionMeta(TxnID)` — used for admin inspection. The PIP should either specify these methods' metadata-driven behavior or explain why they can throw `UnsupportedOperationException`.

- [pip-473.md:~205] `appendBufferToTxn(txnId, buf)` signature does not match the interface: `appendBufferToTxn(TxnID txnId, long sequenceId, ByteBuf buffer)`. The `sequenceId` parameter is used by the dispatcher to match entries to ops; the design should clarify whether this maps to the metadata-store `<seq>` or remains independent.

- [pip-473.md:~215] `isTxnAborted(msg)` should be `isTxnAborted(TxnID txnID, Position readPosition)`. The interface takes a `Position` argument so the TB can check whether a specific entry falls within an aborted transaction's write range.

- [pip-473.md:~210] `commit(txnId, position)` / `abort(...)` should be `commitTxn(TxnID, long lowWaterMark)` / `abortTxn(TxnID, long lowWaterMark)`. The PIP states these are "not invoked by the v5 TC" — but they must still exist on the implementation. The PIP should clarify that they are no-ops or throw `UnsupportedOperationException`, and that callers on the v4 path will never reach them for `segment://` topics (enforced by the provider routing).

- [pip-473.md:~218] `recover()` should map to `checkIfTBRecoverCompletely()` which returns `CompletableFuture<Void>`. The PIP correctly describes the no-log-replay semantics but should name the actual interface method for implementers.

- [pip-473.md:~255] `MetadataPendingAckStore` describes `commit(txnId)` / `abort(txnId)`, but the `PendingAckStore` interface uses `appendCommitMark(TxnID, AckType)` and `appendAbortMark(TxnID, AckType)`. Same "not invoked by v5 TC" clarification needed.

- [pip-473.md:~105-115] The PIP proposes extending `MetadataStore` with partition-key co-location, sequential keys, secondary indexes, and **range watches**. Current capabilities: sequential keys exist (`CreateOption.Sequential`), secondary indexes exist (`findByIndex` + `put` with `Map<String, String>`), but **partition-key co-location and range watches do not exist**. The `registerListener(Consumer<Notification>)` interface supports only point notifications (Created/Modified/Deleted per path). The PIP should provide the proposed method signatures for these extensions (e.g., `registerRangeWatch(String indexName, SecondaryKey key, Consumer<Notification>)`) and acknowledge that range-watch support is the most invasive interface change.

- [pip-473.md:~305-330] The PIP states `addPartitionToTxn` and `addSubscriptionToTxn` are "no-op at the coordinator." Currently, the client uses `ADD_PARTITION_TO_TXN` to register the TC-to-participant routing table, and the TC uses this to fan out `END_TXN_ON_PARTITION` RPCs. If the v5 TC never tracks participants, the client still needs to know which broker owns a segment to send its write/ack. The PIP should explain how `ADD_PARTITION_TO_TXN` is handled on the v5 path — is it a true no-op at the TC (client sends it, TC ignores it), or does the client skip it entirely? The latter requires a client-side wire-protocol change not mentioned in "Binary protocol: No changes."

- [pip-473.md:~115] The header state model uses three states (`OPEN`, `COMMITTED`, `ABORTED`), eliminating the intermediate `COMMITTING` and `ABORTING` states from the existing `TxnStatus` enum. This is a deliberate simplification but has implications: the current `TxnMeta` tracks `COMMITTING`/`ABORTING` as transient states to prevent concurrent end-txn attempts. A three-state model with CAS (`OPEN → COMMITTED|ABORTED`) is simpler, but the PIP should acknowledge this explicitly as a protocol-level divergence between v4 and v5 transactions, even if the wire commands are the same.

- [pip-473.md:~360-365] The PIP mentions "per-namespace or per-broker setting selects the TB implementation" and that defaults differ by topic domain. However, the current architecture has **a single global provider** (`PulsarService.transactionBufferProvider`, configured once in `ServiceConfiguration.transactionBufferProviderClassName`). There is no per-topic-domain routing. The PIP should specify whether the provider itself inspects `TopicName.isSegment()` to dispatch (simplest), or whether a new configuration mechanism (per-namespace provider selection) is proposed. Without this, the implementation path is underspecified.

- [pip-473.md:~145-155] The `idx:txn-by-deadline` and `idx:txn-by-final-state` secondary indexes require range-query-and-watch semantics. For ZooKeeper (which lacks native secondary indexes), the PIP says it "can implement them in a less efficient way (client-maintained index records; periodic re-list in lieu of range-watch)." This is a significant operational concern: the GC sweep and timeout abort sweep depend on these indexes. The PIP should add a risk note that ZooKeeper-backed brokers may have degraded transaction GC/timeout performance, potentially blocking upgrades for clusters still on ZK.

- [pip-473.md:~380] The PIP states "Existing `pulsar_txn_tb_*` snapshot/replay metrics are not emitted by the new implementation." Callers that rely on these metrics for monitoring (e.g., TB recovery duration, snapshot lag) will see a metrics gap for `segment://` topics. The new metrics listed (`pulsar_txn_metadata_store_*`) are good additions, but the PIP should note which existing dashboards/alerts will need updating to accommodate the metrics discontinuity.

- [pip-473.md:~230] `getStats(boolean, boolean)` and `getStats(boolean)` return `TransactionBufferStats` which includes `ongoingTransactions`, `abortedTransactions`, `committedTransactions`, and per-ledger stats. The metadata-driven TB needs to report these from metadata-store queries (header count, index counts). The PIP's metrics section lists `pulsar_txn_metadata_store_outstanding_op_records` but does not address the structured stats object used by existing admin APIs (`pulsar-admin transactions stats`).

SUMMARY:
This PR adds `systemTopicDefaultNumPartitions` config to isolate system topic partition counts from regular topics. While the motivation is valid, the implementation has several breaking issues: mandatory validation on a new field without CLI support, potential NPE from Integer unboxing with legacy policies, and backward compatibility problems with existing stored namespace policies.

POINTS:
- [pulsar-client-admin-api/src/main/java/org/apache/pulsar/common/policies/data/impl/AutoTopicCreationOverrideImpl.java:53-60] **Breaking validation change** - The PR makes `systemTopicDefaultNumPartitions` mandatory when topic type is PARTITIONED. This breaks existing CLI usage (`pulsar-admin namespaces set-auto-topic-creation --type partitioned --num-partitions N`) since CmdNamespaces.java has no `--system-topic-num-partitions` option. Existing tests (`AutoTopicCreationOverrideTest.testValidOverridePartitioned`, `NamespacesTest`, `BrokerServiceChaosTest`) build overrides with only `defaultNumPartitions` and will fail this validation. **Recommendation**: Make this field optional (defaulting to `defaultNumPartitions` when null), not mandatory.

- [pulsar-broker/src/main/java/org/apache/pulsar/broker/service/BrokerService.java:3732-3733] **Potential NPE from Integer unboxing** - `autoTopicCreationOverride.getSystemTopicDefaultNumPartitions()` returns `Integer` (nullable), but `getDefaultNumPartitions()` returns primitive `int`. Existing namespace policies stored in ZooKeeper will NOT have this field in their JSON, so Jackson deserializes it as `null`. When `isSystemTopic()` returns true for an override-loaded policy, unboxing null → NPE. **Recommendation**: Add null-fallback: `autoTopicCreationOverride.getSystemTopicDefaultNumPartitions() != null ? autoTopicCreationOverride.getSystemTopicDefaultNumPartitions() : autoTopicCreationOverride.getDefaultNumPartitions()` or similar fallback to `pulsar.getConfiguration().getDefaultNumPartitions()`.

- [pulsar-client-admin-api/src/main/java/org/apache/pulsar/common/policies/data/impl/AutoTopicCreationOverrideImpl.java:58-60] **Validation inconsistency** - The PR validates that `systemTopicDefaultNumPartitions` cannot be ≤0 for partitioned topics, but does NOT add corresponding validation in the NON_PARTITIONED branch (unlike how `defaultNumPartitions` is rejected for non-partitioned at line 63-65). Should also reject `systemTopicDefaultNumPartitions != null` for non-partitioned types for symmetry.

- [pulsar-client-admin-api/src/main/java/org/apache/pulsar/common/policies/data/AutoTopicCreationOverride.java:32] **Missing CLI support** - The `AutoTopicCreationOverride.Builder` interface gains `systemTopicDefaultNumPartitions()`, but no corresponding CLI option is added in `CmdNamespaces.java`. Users cannot configure this namespace-level override through the admin CLI, making the namespace-level feature unusable. **Recommendation**: Add CLI option or document that this is only configurable at broker level.

- [pulsar-broker-common/src/main/java/org/apache/pulsar/broker/ServiceConfiguration.java:2345-2350] **Documentation improvement** - The field doc "Default number of partitions for the system topics" is vague. **Suggestion**: Expand to clarify purpose, e.g., "Default number of partitions for system topics. Isolates system topic partition counts from user-defined topics to prevent resource waste when defaultNumPartitions is set high."

- [pulsar-broker/src/test/java/org/apache/pulsar/broker/systopic/PartitionedSystemTopicTest.java:83] **Test coverage gap** - The test sets `systemTopicDefaultNumPartitions` to the same value as `defaultNumPartitions` (both use `PARTITIONS`), so it cannot actually verify that the two configs are independent. **Recommendation**: Add a test case where `defaultNumPartitions` and `systemTopicDefaultNumPartitions` differ, and verify that system topics get the system-specific partition count while regular topics get the default.

SUMMARY:
This PR adds a new mojo to modify P2 repository metadata (name and properties). The implementation is functional and thread-safe, but has DI convention mismatches with existing mojos in tycho-p2-repository-plugin, uses fragile internal Eclipse APIs, and has a few minor issues including a potential NPE and typo.

POINTS:
- [tycho-p2-repository-plugin/src/main/java/org/eclipse/tycho/plugins/p2/repository/ModifyRepositoryPropertiesMojo.java:125-127] Uses `@Inject` for dependency injection (`P2RepositoryDataManipulator`, `Logger`), but existing mojos in this plugin (e.g., `AssembleRepositoryMojo`, `FixArtifactsMetadataMetadataMojo`) exclusively use `@Component`. This inconsistency could cause DI resolution issues — align with plugin convention and use `@Component`.

- [tycho-p2-repository-plugin/src/main/java/org/eclipse/tycho/plugins/p2/repository/ModifyRepositoryPropertiesMojo.java:61-62] The `repository` parameter lacks `required = true`. Without it, the mojo can execute without a repository configured, leading to a `NullPointerException` when `createDescriptor()` is called at line 113. Add `@Parameter(required = true)` to fail fast with a clear Maven configuration error.

- [p2-maven-plugin/src/main/java/org/eclipse/tycho/p2maven/tools/P2RepositoryDataManipulator.java:82] Unconditionally sets `PROP_COMPRESSED` even for in-place edits. Existing code in `AbstractApplication.java:194-195` only sets this property if not already present. This overwrites any existing compression setting on the source repository. Consider the same guard pattern or document this as intentional overwrite behavior.

- [p2-maven-plugin/src/main/java/org/eclipse/tycho/p2maven/repository/P2RepositoryManager.java:24-27] Imports and uses `org.eclipse.equinox.internal.p2.*` APIs (`SimpleArtifactRepositoryFactory`, `SimpleMetadataRepositoryFactory`, `SimpleArtifactRepository`). These are Eclipse internal APIs that can change without notice between Eclipse releases. Consider whether the public P2 repository SPIs could be used instead, or document the Eclipse version dependency explicitly.

- [p2-maven-plugin/src/main/java/org/eclipse/tycho/p2maven/tools/P2RepositoryDataManipulator.java:119] Typo in error message: `"Repository is not modifable and no output location is defined"` — should be `"modifiable"`.

- [p2-maven-plugin/src/main/java/org/eclipse/tycho/p2maven/repository/P2RepositoryManager.java:210] `assertNoError` is declared `public static` but is only called from within `P2RepositoryManager` itself (lines 125, 169). Make it `private static` to reduce public API surface.

- [p2-maven-plugin/src/main/java/org/eclipse/tycho/p2maven/repository/P2RepositoryManager.java:128-130] Stream pipeline in `mirrorArtifactRepositoryData` reuses `a` as both the map lambda parameter and the forEach parameter: `.map(a -> repository.getArtifactDescriptors(a)).forEach(a -> mirror.addDescriptors(a, null))`. Rename for clarity, e.g., `key` for the artifact key and `descriptors` for the array.

- [p2-maven-plugin/src/main/java/org/eclipse/tycho/p2maven/repository/P2RepositoryManager.java:166] In `mirrorMetadataRepository`, `.map(List::of).forEach(mirror::addInstallableUnits)` — `List::of` creates an immutable single-element list. Verify that `IMetadataRepository.addInstallableUnits` accepts this pattern; consider `repository.query(QueryUtil.ALL_UNITS, null).forEach(mirror::addInstallableUnit)` if single-unit add exists, or clarify intent in a comment.

SUMMARY:
This PR refactors scheduler OpenTelemetry instrumentation from build-time `@WithSpan` annotation weaving to runtime instrumentation via a new `JobInstrumenter` SPI. While the architecture is sound and integration tests are comprehensive, there are several concerning issues around mandatory OTel dependencies in scheduler-common, fire-and-forget async patterns that may break Quartz exception propagation, an acknowledged context propagation bug, and existing tests/documentation not addressed.

POINTS:
- [extensions/scheduler/common/pom.xml:17-30] The PR adds `quarkus-scheduler-spi` and `opentelemetry-instrumentation-api` as **mandatory (non-optional)** dependencies to `scheduler-common`. This means every project using `quarkus-scheduler` or `quarkus-quartz` will transitively depend on OpenTelemetry instrumentation APIs even when `quarkus.scheduler.tracing.enabled=false` or when OTel is not present. These should be `<optional>true</optional>` to avoid forcing OTel transitive dependencies on all scheduler users.

- [extensions/quartz/runtime/src/main/java/io/quarkus/quartz/runtime/InstrumentedJob.java:30-42] The `execute()` method is `void` but `instrumenter.instrument()` returns `CompletionStage<Void>` that is **never consumed** (fire-and-forget). More critically, when the delegate throws `JobExecutionException`, it is caught and wrapped in `CompletableFuture.failedFuture(e)`, but this CompletionStage is disconnected from Quartz's execution model. Quartz expects `JobExecutionException` to propagate so it can handle retries, misfired job tracking, and error logging. Consider re-throwing `JobExecutionException` from the catch block: `if (e instanceof JobExecutionException) throw (JobExecutionException) e;`.

- [extensions/scheduler/common/src/main/java/io/quarkus/scheduler/common/runtime/InstrumentedInvoker.java:28-35] Same fire-and-forget pattern: `delegate.invoke()` can throw checked exceptions per the `ScheduledInvoker` interface contract (`throws Exception`), but the catch block wraps them in `CompletableFuture.failedFuture(e)` and the returned CompletionStage is never awaited. This breaks the exception propagation contract for the invoker chain. The `StatusEmitterInvoker` and other invokers in the chain expect either a completed CompletionStage OR a thrown exception.

- [PR description] The author acknowledges: "For some reason with my implementation I cannot log traceId and spanId inside the executed method even tho span has been created (gonna have a look at it in a meantime)." This is a **functional gap** — if the span context is not visible inside the scheduled method, users cannot correlate logs with traces. This should be resolved before merging. The root cause may be related to the fire-and-forget pattern or context propagation on Quartz's thread pool.

- [extensions/scheduler/deployment/src/test/java/io/quarkus/scheduler/test/tracing/OpenTelemetryTracingTest.java] This existing test validates span creation when `quarkus.scheduler.tracing.enabled=true`, but it's NOT in the changed files. The PR removes the build-time `@WithSpan` injection approach that this test was designed for. Verify this test still passes with the new runtime instrumentation approach, or update it to reflect the new mechanism.

- [docs/src/main/asciidoc/scheduler-reference.adoc:418-420] The documentation still describes the OLD approach: "the `@io.opentelemetry.instrumentation.annotations.WithSpan` annotation is added automatically to every `@Scheduled` method." This needs to be updated to reflect the new runtime instrumentation model via `JobInstrumenter` SPI.

- [extensions/opentelemetry/deployment/src/main/java/io/quarkus/opentelemetry/deployment/scheduler/OpenTelemetrySchedulerProcessor.java:13-16] The build step registers `OpenTelemetryJobInstrumenter` as a CDI bean only when both `OpenTelemetryEnabled` and `Capability.SCHEDULER` are present. However, consider whether the registration should also respect `quarkus.scheduler.tracing.enabled` to avoid registering the instrumenter when tracing is explicitly disabled.

- [extensions/quartz/runtime/src/main/java/io/quarkus/quartz/runtime/InstrumentedJob.java:45] vs [extensions/scheduler/common/src/main/java/io/quarkus/scheduler/common/runtime/InstrumentedInvoker.java:40] Inconsistent span naming: Quartz uses `key.getGroup() + '.' + key.getName()` (e.g., "myGroup.myManualJob"), while Simple Scheduler uses `execution.getTrigger().getId()` (e.g., "myCounter"). Consider aligning these conventions or documenting the difference.

- [extensions/opentelemetry/runtime/src/main/java/io/quarkus/opentelemetry/runtime/scheduler/OpenTelemetryJobInstrumenter.java:14-20] The `SpanNameExtractor` and `ErrorCauseExtractor` are defined as anonymous inner classes that could be simplified. The `ErrorCauseExtractor` simply returns the throwable unchanged — verify this is the intended behavior per OTel instrumentation API conventions.

- [extensions/scheduler/deployment/src/main/java/io/quarkus/scheduler/deployment/SchedulerProcessor.java] The PR removes the entire `tracing()` build step method (lines 370-392). Ensure all references to the removed `@WithSpan` annotation transformation are cleaned up and that the `SchedulerDotNames.SCHEDULED_NAME` and `SCHEDULES_NAME` constants are no longer needed for tracing purposes.

SUMMARY:
This PR refactors the preferences system by replacing the `preferences` package with a new `settings` package using an interface-based design (`SettingsEditor`/`SettingsAsPreferencesEditor`), removing duplicate GUI-specific preference classes, and consolidating job/history retrieval into `JobService`. While the architectural goals are sound, the migration introduces two breaking changes that will silently degrade existing user experience: loss of the `ALWAYS_AUTO_PASTE` preference and corruption of persisted `Folders` JSON data.

POINTS:
- [Core/src/main/java/settings/SettingsEditor.java, Core/src/main/java/settings/AppSettings.java] **`ALWAYS_AUTO_PASTE` preference silently dropped.** The old `gui.preferences.Labels` enum included `ALWAYS_AUTO_PASTE`, and `UIController.isAutoPaste()` returned `form.cbAutoPaste.isSelected() || AppSettings.GET.alwaysAutoPaste()`, providing a global override that forced auto-paste ON regardless of the checkbox. The new settings system has no equivalent method. After migration, users who relied on `ALWAYS_AUTO_PASTE` will lose that behavior; auto-paste will depend solely on the per-session checkbox. Either add `isAlwaysAutoPaste()`/`setAlwaysAutoPaste()` to `SettingsEditor` and `AppSettings`, or explicitly document this as intentional feature removal.

- [GUI/src/main/java/gui/support/Folders.java:66–80] **Folders JSON serialization format change breaks existing user data.** The old code serialized the `Folders` object: `gson.toJson(folders)` → `{"folders":["/path/1","/path/2"]}`. The new code serializes the `LinkedList<String>` directly: `GSON.toJson(folders)` → `["/path/1","/path/2"]`. The new `readFolders()` method calls `GSON.fromJson(json, String[].class)`, which throws `JsonSyntaxException` when encountering the old `{"folders":[...]}` format. The exception is caught but only logged — the user's saved folder list silently becomes empty. Add backward compatibility: attempt to parse as `String[]` first, and on `JsonSyntaxException`, try parsing as a wrapper object with a `folders` field (or use `JsonElement` to inspect whether the root is an array or object).

- [Core/src/main/java/support/DownloadConfiguration.java:270–271, GUI/src/main/java/ui/UIController.java:130–134,146–150,507–511,519–523,576–580,635–641] **Commented-out dead code from Jobs manipulation.** The PR correctly identifies that `JobService.getJobs()` returns a fresh `Jobs` object each call, making `.setList()`, `.remove()`, `.add()`, `.clear()` calls ineffective. However, the old code was identically broken — `preferences.Get.jobs()` also created a new object per call. The commented-out blocks with `/* no effect ... */` annotations are dead code clutter. Consider either: (a) removing the commented blocks entirely and updating the design so `JobService` returns a managed singleton that persists changes, or (b) documenting the known limitation more clearly. Leaving commented-out code with explanations is not better than leaving the broken working code.

- [Core/src/main/java/settings/SettingsAsPreferencesEditor.java:164–170] **`flush()` called only on `setDriftyUpdateAvailable()`.** The old code implicitly relied on `Preferences` auto-flushing or periodic saves. The new code calls `PREFERENCES.flush()` explicitly only inside `setDriftyUpdateAvailable()` (line 167). All other setters skip flushing, relying on JVM termination to persist. If the application crashes before JVM shutdown, settings like theme, folders, auto-paste, etc. will be lost. Consider flushing in a general `save()` method or using `Preferences.addPreferenceChangeListener()` to auto-flush on changes.

- [GUI/src/main/java/ui/UIController.java:609] **`isAutoPaste()` no longer checks persistent preference.** Now returns only `form.cbAutoPaste.isSelected()`. Combined with the `ALWAYS_AUTO_PASTE` removal, the checkbox state is purely ephemeral — toggling it in one session does not persist to the next. The `Settings.java` listener (`createAutoPasteCheck()`) calls `AppSettings.setGuiAutoPasteEnabled(newValue)` on toggle, but `isAutoPaste()` ignores this value. The checkbox's persisted state in settings is disconnected from the runtime check.

- [Core/src/main/java/module-info.java] **`exports data` added for new `JobService` package.** The `data` package is newly exported, but only `JobService` is defined there. Verify that `data.JobService` is the intended public API for both CLI and GUI modules, or consider restricting exports if this is an internal implementation detail.

SUMMARY:
This PR introduces CRAC (Coordinated Restore at Checkpoint) configuration options, class preloading via META-INF files, and full warmup support. The design is reasonable but has several code quality issues including dead code, thread-safety concerns with recorder static fields, and overly broad exception swallowing.

POINTS:
- [core/deployment/src/main/java/io/quarkus/deployment/CracProcessor.java:30] Dead null check: `defaultVal == null` can never be true. The Quarkus builder framework wraps Optional parameters with `Optional.ofNullable()` (see `ExtensionLoader.java:354-358`), guaranteeing a non-null Optional. The condition should be simplified to `!defaultVal.map(CracDefaultValueBuildItem::isDefaultValue).orElse(false)` or at minimum `!defaultVal.isPresent() || !defaultVal.get().isDefaultValue()`.

- [core/runtime/src/main/java/io/quarkus/runtime/CracRecorder.java:45-46] Recorder static fields violate Quarkus conventions: `enabled` and `fullWarmup` are declared `public static boolean` but existing recorders use `private static volatile` (see `ExecutorRecorder.java:28`, `ShutdownRecorder.java:18`, `GrpcServerRecorder.java:82-83`, `RestClientRecorder.java:11`). These fields are written during static init and read later in `Quarkus.manualInitialize()` — without `volatile` there is no memory visibility guarantee across threads. Recommend: `private static volatile boolean enabled;` and `private static volatile boolean fullWarmup;`.

- [core/runtime/src/main/java/io/quarkus/runtime/Quarkus.java:248,253] `manualState = MANUAL_INITIALIZED` is set twice: once at line 248 after instantiating `manualApp`, and again at line 253 after CRAC registration. The second assignment is redundant and obscures the actual state progression. If CRAC registration fails (throws), the state would incorrectly remain `MANUAL_INITIALIZED` instead of transitioning to `MANUAL_FAILURE` — though the surrounding try/catch does handle this. Remove the redundant line 253.

- [core/runtime/src/main/java/io/quarkus/runtime/PreloadClassesRecorder.java:25] `catch (Throwable ignored)` silently swallows ALL throwables including `OutOfMemoryError`, `StackOverflowError`, and `VirtualMachineError`. This makes debugging class loading failures impossible. Recommend narrowing to `catch (ClassNotFoundException | LinkageError e)` and at minimum logging a debug message.

- [core/runtime/src/main/java/io/quarkus/runtime/PreloadClassesRecorder.java:36,62] IOException is silently ignored in `preloadClasses()` and `preloadClassesFromStream()`. While the try-with-resources pattern handles resource cleanup, silently ignoring `IOException` when reading `META-INF/quarkus-preload-classes.txt` files means misconfigured preload files go unnoticed. Consider at least a debug-level log.

- [core/deployment/src/main/java/io/quarkus/deployment/builditem/CracEnabledBuildItem.java] This marker build item is produced by `CracProcessor.processCrac()` at line 32 but is not consumed by any other build step in the codebase. If the intent is to let other extensions react to CRAC being enabled, document that expectation; otherwise remove it to avoid confusion.

- [core/deployment/src/main/java/io/quarkus/deployment/steps/PreloadClassesBuildStep.java:24] `registerPreInitClasses` uses `System.lineSeparator()` to join class names, but the reader in `PreloadClassesRecorder.preloadClassesFromStream()` uses `BufferedReader.readLine()` which handles `\n`, `\r`, and `\r\n`. This works correctly but `System.lineSeparator()` is platform-dependent — consider using `\n` (LF) consistently since these are generated resources embedded in JARs where platform conventions are irrelevant.

- [core/runtime/src/main/java/io/quarkus/runtime/PreloadClassesRecorder.java:56] The try-with-resources declaration has unusual formatting with the resource list on separate lines (`try (is; InputStreamReader isr = ...; BufferedReader reader = ...)`). This is valid Java but deviates from the project's formatting conventions where resources are typically declared on a single line or with clear alignment.

SUMMARY:
This PR adds support for Bitnami PostgreSQL environment variable keys (`POSTGRESQL_USERNAME`, `POSTGRESQL_DATABASE`) and refactors `PostgresEnvironment` to use arrays with loop-based extraction. However, it introduces two correctness issues: adding unsupported keys for the official postgres image and removing backward compatibility for a valid Bitnami legacy key.

POINTS:
- [spring-boot-project/spring-boot-docker-compose/src/main/java/org/springframework/boot/docker/compose/service/connection/postgres/PostgresEnvironment.java:32-33] `POSTGRES_USERNAME` and `POSTGRES_DATABASE` are added as candidate username/database keys, but these environment variables are **not supported by any known PostgreSQL Docker image**. The official `postgres` image uses `POSTGRES_USER` and `POSTGRES_DB`; the Bitnami image uses `POSTGRESQL_*` variants. Consider removing these from the candidate arrays — they appear to be a prefix mismatch (confusing `POSTGRES_` with `POSTGRESQL_`).

- [spring-boot-project/spring-boot-docker-compose/src/main/java/org/springframework/boot/docker/compose/service/connection/postgres/PostgresEnvironment.java:37-38] `POSTGRESQL_DB` was removed from the `DATABASE_KEYS` array. Bitnami PostgreSQL documentation documents `POSTGRESQL_DB` as a legacy alias for `POSTGRESQL_DATABASE` that is still supported. Removing it is a **backward compatibility regression** for users with existing compose files that use `POSTGRESQL_DB`. Consider keeping it in the candidate array alongside `POSTGRESQL_DATABASE`.

- [spring-boot-project/spring-boot-docker-compose/src/main/java/org/springframework/boot/docker/compose/service/connection/postgres/PostgresEnvironment.java:32-38] The array + loop pattern for extracting candidate keys deviates from the established convention in this module. Other `*Environment` classes (e.g., `MariaDbEnvironment`, `RabbitEnvironment`, `MongoEnvironment`) use chained `getOrDefault` or sequential null-check assignments. While the array approach is arguably cleaner for 4 candidates, consider whether this divergence is intentional or if the change should follow the existing pattern (sequential null checks like `MariaDbEnvironment.extractPassword()`).

- [spring-boot-project/spring-boot-docker-compose/src/test/java/org/springframework/boot/docker/compose/service/connection/postgres/PostgresEnvironmentTests.java:141-147] The test method was renamed from `getDatabaseWhenHasPostgresqlDb` to `getDatabaseWhenHasPostgresqlDatabase`, and the test value changed from `POSTGRESQL_DB` to `POSTGRESQL_DATABASE`. Since `POSTGRESQL_DB` was previously supported and is still valid for Bitnami, consider adding a separate test case for `POSTGRESQL_DB` to prevent future regression if backward compatibility is preserved.

SUMMARY:
PR adds JSON output to the scan command but claims CSV support that is not implemented; also changes output format, error handling patterns, and declares dependencies not present in pom.xml.

POINTS:
- [modules/core/src/main/java/org/apache/fluo/core/util/ScanUtil.java:142-163] **PR title claims CSV support but only JSON is implemented.** No `--csv` flag exists, no CSV generation code exists, and commons-csv is not added to any pom.xml file (investigated all 11 pom.xml files). PR description also claims "Added 2 dependencies: commons-csv:1.5 and commons-lang3:3.7" but neither dependency appears in any module's pom.xml. Please implement CSV output or remove CSV from the PR title/description.

- [modules/core/src/main/java/org/apache/fluo/core/util/ScanUtil.java:132-140] **Breaking change to default scan output format.** The original `scanFluo` used `Hex.encNonAscii(sb, rcv.getColumn(), " ")` which produces `family:qualifier:visibility` format. The new code separates these into space-delimited fields: `family qualifier visibility`. This changes `row family:qualifier:visibility\tvalue` to `row family qualifier visibility\tvalue`, breaking any scripts that parse existing output format.

- [modules/core/src/main/java/org/apache/fluo/core/util/ScanUtil.java:198] **Error handling regression in scanAccumulo.** Original code caught Exception, printed to stderr, and called `System.exit(-1)`. New code catches Exception and wraps it in `throw new RuntimeException(e)`. This changes behavior: callers must explicitly catch RuntimeException, and the exception will propagate with a stack trace rather than a clean error message. AppRunner and FluoScan have try-catch blocks, but if scanAccumulo is ever called from other contexts, this error handling change is unexpected.

- [modules/core/src/main/java/org/apache/fluo/core/util/ScanUtil.java:165-183] **scanAccumulo does not support JSON export despite accepting exportAsJson in ScanOpts.** The `generateJson()` method is only called from `scanFluo()`. When `--raw` is used (scanAccumuloTable=true), the JSON flag is blocked by `FluoScan.checkScanOptions()`, but `scanAccumulo` itself silently ignores the `exportAsJson` field. Consider either implementing JSON for raw mode or documenting the limitation.

- [modules/core/src/main/java/org/apache/fluo/core/util/ScanUtil.java:151] **Gson `setVersion(1.0)` has no effect.** The `generateJson` method serializes a plain `LinkedHashMap<String, String>`, so the `.setVersion(1.0)` call on GsonBuilder is meaningless — `@Since` annotations work only on class fields, not on generic Map entries. This configuration can be removed.

- [modules/core/src/main/java/org/apache/fluo/core/util/ScanUtil.java:153-163] **LinkedHashMap reused across iterations in generateJson.** The same `json` map instance is populated and serialized for each record. While technically correct since Gson reads the current state each call, this is a code smell — a new map should be created per record for clarity and to avoid potential issues if the serialization logic changes.

- [modules/core/src/main/java/org/apache/fluo/core/util/ScanUtil.java:108] **scanFluo and scanAccumulo have no unit tests.** Existing ScanTest.java (modules/command/src/test/java/...) only tests argument parsing via `getSpan()` and `getColumns()`. Neither `scanFluo()` nor `scanAccumulo()` is tested. The new JSON output path and the output format change should have test coverage.

- [modules/command/src/main/java/org/apache/fluo/command/FluoScan.java:87-91] **Error message in checkScanOptions references wrong flag name.** The message says "Both \"--raw\" and \"--json\" can not be set together" which is correct, but consider whether this should allow the combination and instead implement JSON output for `scanAccumulo` as noted above.

SUMMARY:
This PR introduces the `pulsar-client-api-v5` module implementing PIP-466, providing type-safe interfaces for scalable topics including Producer, StreamConsumer, QueueConsumer, and CheckpointConsumer with sync/async variants, schema, auth, and config packages. The API design is generally clean and well-documented, but there are several inconsistencies across interfaces and with v4 conventions that should be addressed before merge.

POINTS:
- [pulsar-client-api-v5/src/main/java/org/apache/pulsar/client/api/v5/Producer.java:40] Inconsistent `Closeable` vs `AutoCloseable` inheritance: `Producer` and `QueueConsumer` extend `AutoCloseable`, while `StreamConsumer` and `CheckpointConsumer` extend `Closeable`. The existing v4 API (`pulsar-client-api/src/main/java/org/apache/pulsar/client/api/Producer.java:36` and `Consumer.java:37`) uses `Closeable` for both. Decide on a consistent choice — `Closeable` is preferred if the close methods can throw `PulsarClientException` (a subclass of `IOException`), since `AutoCloseable.close()` declares only `Exception` and calling code loses the more specific exception type in try-with-resources.

- [pulsar-client-api-v5/src/main/java/org/apache/pulsar/client/api/v5/QueueConsumer.java:61] `QueueConsumer` is missing the `consumerName()` accessor that `StreamConsumer` provides (`StreamConsumer.java:58`). Both are managed consumers with subscription identity; `consumerName` is a useful diagnostic property that should be present on all managed consumer types.

- [pulsar-client-api-v5/src/main/java/org/apache/pulsar/client/api/v5/async/AsyncCheckpointConsumer.java:57] Async `receiveMulti` returns `CompletableFuture<List<Message<T>>>`, losing the `lastId()` method available on the sync `Messages<T>` return type. Consider returning `CompletableFuture<Messages<T>>` to preserve the last-ID capability needed for cumulative ack tracking, or if keeping `List`, document why `lastId()` is unavailable and whether consumers need to track the last message ID themselves. Same applies to `AsyncStreamConsumer.java:55`.

- [pulsar-client-api-v5/src/main/java/org/apache/pulsar/client/api/v5/async/AsyncQueueConsumer.java:45-51] `AsyncQueueConsumer` has `acknowledge(Message<T> message)` and `negativeAcknowledge(Message<T> message)` convenience overloads that the sync `QueueConsumer` lacks. If these are intentional async-only conveniences (justifiable since async callers already hold the `Message` object from the future), add a note in the javadoc explaining why they don't appear on the sync counterpart.

- [pulsar-client-api-v5/src/main/java/org/apache/pulsar/client/api/v5/QueueConsumerBuilder.java:78] `receiverQueueSize(int)` is present on `QueueConsumerBuilder` but absent from `StreamConsumerBuilder`. In the v4 API, `receiverQueueSize` is on the shared `ConsumerBuilder` interface (`pulsar-client-api/src/main/java/org/apache/pulsar/client/api/ConsumerBuilder.java:423`). For stream consumers, receiver queue size controls prefetch depth which affects latency vs throughput tradeoffs. Consider adding it to `StreamConsumerBuilder` or explicitly document why it's not applicable for stream consumers.

- [pulsar-client-api-v5/src/main/java/org/apache/pulsar/client/api/v5/config/ChunkingPolicy.java:27] `ChunkingPolicy` record lacks static factory methods. Other config records like `BatchingPolicy` (`ofDefault()`, `ofDisabled()`), `CompressionPolicy` (`disabled()`, `of()`), and `DeadLetterPolicy` (`of(int)`) all provide convenience factories — `ChunkingPolicy` has none, so consumers must construct it directly via the record constructor. Add `disabled()` and `ofEnabled(int)` factory methods for consistency.

- [pulsar-client-api-v5/src/main/java/org/apache/pulsar/client/api/v5/CheckpointConsumer.java:77] Parameter name inconsistency: `CheckpointConsumer.receiveMulti` uses `maxMessages` while `StreamConsumer.receiveMulti` uses `maxNumMessages`. Same semantic, different name — unify to one naming convention.

- [pulsar-client-api-v5/src/main/java/org/apache/pulsar/client/api/v5/PulsarClientException.java:34] The exception hierarchy uses nested `static final class` types without `serialVersionUID` fields. The v4 `PulsarClientException` uses `@SuppressWarnings("serial")` to suppress the compiler warning — this PR's version has no such annotation, which will generate compiler warnings. Either add `@SuppressWarnings("serial")` or declare `serialVersionUID` fields on each nested exception class.

- [pulsar-client-api-v5/build.gradle.kts:23] `compileOnly` dependencies on `protobuf-java` and `opentelemetry-api` mean that any downstream module using this API must also declare those dependencies transitively. The v4 `pulsar-client-api` has no external runtime dependencies (it's a pure Java API module). Consider whether protobuf types are truly needed at the API boundary or whether the API should remain protobuf-free, delegating serialization to the implementation module.

- [pulsar-client-api-v5/src/main/java/org/apache/pulsar/client/api/v5/internal/PulsarClientProvider.java:108] The `get()` method uses the Holder pattern for thread-safe lazy initialization, which is correct. However, the `IllegalStateException` thrown when no provider is found is unchecked — consider whether this should be wrapped in a `PulsarClientException.InvalidConfigurationException` for consistency with the rest of the API's exception hierarchy.

SUMMARY:
This PR implements comprehensive OpenTelemetry tracing for the MCP server following OTel semantic conventions. The design is well-structured with proper build-time conditional bean registration, runtime config checks, and minimal overhead when disabled. Key concerns include scope propagation to worker threads, multi-server tracing configuration granularity, and a couple of minor code issues.

POINTS:
- [core/runtime/src/main/java/io/quarkiverse/mcp/server/runtime/FeatureManagerBase.java:344-372] OTel scope (ThreadLocal-based) is activated on the event loop thread but does NOT propagate to worker threads when `@Tool` methods execute via `vertx.executeBlocking()`. User instrumentation inside `@Tool` methods that call `Span.current()` to create child spans will create independent spans instead of nested children. Consider propagating the `Context` explicitly through `ArgumentProviders` or using `ContextStorage` attachment for worker thread execution.

- [core/runtime/src/main/java/io/quarkiverse/mcp/server/runtime/tracing/McpTracingInstrumenter.java:65] The instrumenter captures the tracing enabled state from only `McpServer.DEFAULT` at construction time: `mcpConfig.servers().get(McpServer.DEFAULT).tracingEnabled()`. In multi-server setups, this means tracing cannot be toggled per-server — it's global. Since `McpTracingInstrumenter` is `@Singleton`, all servers share the same enabled/disabled state. Consider either documenting this limitation or making the enabled check per-request based on `mcpRequest.serverName()`.

- [core/runtime/src/main/java/io/quarkiverse/mcp/server/runtime/tracing/McpTracingInstrumenter.java:65] The `.setEnabled()` flag is evaluated once during instrumenter construction. If `quarkus.mcp.server.tracing.enabled` is toggled at runtime (via smallrye-config dynamic properties), the change will NOT take effect until the bean is recreated. This is a deviation from how other Quarkus observability features handle runtime toggling. Consider storing a reference to the config object and re-evaluating the enabled state in `shouldStart()`.

- [test/src/main/java/io/quarkiverse/mcp/server/test/McpStreamableTestClientImpl.java:254] `BatchAssert.doSend()` returns null without starting a tracing span, with a `// todo handle otel in batches` comment. This means batch request assertions in tests will have dangling client-side tracing spans if tracing is enabled on the test client. Consider either implementing tracing for batches or documenting that batch assertions don't support client tracing.

- [core/runtime/src/main/java/io/quarkiverse/mcp/server/runtime/FeatureManagerBase.java:96] The `McpTracing` instance is resolved eagerly in the constructor via `Arc.container().select(McpTracing.class)`. This works correctly, but note that this lookup happens before the request context is activated. Verified that `McpTracingInstrumenter` doesn't have any request-scoped dependencies — it injects `OpenTelemetry`, `OTelRuntimeConfig`, and `McpServersRuntimeConfig`, all application-scoped — so this is safe.

- [core/runtime/src/main/java/io/quarkiverse/mcp/server/runtime/McpMessageHandler.java:222] The validation error tracing path (`setTracingErrorResponse(false, JsonRpcErrorCodes.INVALID_REQUEST, "Schema validation failed")`) hardcodes the error message as "Schema validation failed" regardless of the actual validation error detail. The `JsonSchemaValidator` sends the actual error message to the client but the span only records the generic message. Consider passing the actual validation error message to `setTracingErrorResponse()`.

- [core/runtime/src/main/java/io/quarkiverse/mcp/server/runtime/tracing/McpTracingInstrumenter.java:140] The `injectMcpOtelContext(JsonObject meta)` method always injects the current context, even when `instrumenter.shouldStart()` would return false (tracing disabled). This is a minor concern — injection happens regardless of whether the MCP span was started, which means `_meta` will contain trace context headers even when MCP tracing is disabled. This is consistent with Quarkus HTTP behavior (where the HTTP span always exists), but worth noting.

SUMMARY:
This PR introduces a new service installation API supporting non-blocking lifecycle operations via new Lifecycle, BlockingLifecycle, and NonBlockingLifecycle interfaces, deprecating the old UnaryBuilder/AsyncServiceBuilder in favor of BlockingBuilder/NonBlockingBuilder. The design is largely coherent but contains a behavioral regression in CapabilityServiceInstaller's default start mode, a missing blocking instrumentation method on the new API, a misleading javadoc, and several semantic issues worth addressing.

POINTS:
- [subsystem/src/main/java/org/wildfly/subsystem/service/capability/CapabilityServiceInstaller.java:DefaultBlockingBuilder constructor] The new `DefaultBlockingBuilder` does NOT call `withDefaultMode(ON_DEMAND)`. The old `DefaultBuilder` inherited from `AbstractUnaryBuilder` which defaulted to `ServiceController.Mode.ON_DEMAND` (see base Installer.java line ~299). The new class inherits from `AbstractBlockingValueBuilder` → `AbstractBuilder` which defaults to `PASSIVE`. This is a regression: `CapabilityServiceInstaller.BlockingBuilder.of(capability, supplier).build()` now installs with mode PASSIVE instead of ON_DEMAND. The copy constructor used by `map()` correctly calls `withDefaultMode(ON_DEMAND)`, but the primary constructor does not. Callers depending on the old default behavior will see their services no longer starting on-demand.
- [subsystem/src/main/java/org/wildfly/subsystem/service/capability/CapabilityServiceInstaller.java:BlockingBuilder interface] The old `Builder<T,V>` extended `BlockingBuilder<Builder<T,V>>` which declared a `blocking()` method for enabling blocking instrumentation. The new `BlockingBuilder<T,V>` extends `Installer.BlockingValueBuilder` which lacks `blocking()`. Callers using the new API have no way to enable blocking instrumentation — the method is silently lost. Either the new interface should also declare `blocking()` or this is an intentional breaking change that should be documented.
- [service/src/main/java/org/wildfly/service/Lifecycle.java:24] Javadoc says "return true, if this object is started" for method `isStopped()`. Should read "return true, if this object is stopped".
- [service/src/main/java/org/wildfly/service/BlockingLifecycle.java:38-47] `BlockingLifecycle.NONE` returns `false` for both `isStarted()` and `isStopped()`. This means `isStarted() || isStopped()` can be `false`, violating the expected invariant. The composite lifecycle's guards like `if (lifecycle.isStopped()) lifecycle.start()` will skip NONE entirely — correct behavior, but confusing semantics. Consider having NONE return `true` for `isStopped()` (since it was never started) and `false` for `isStarted()`.
- [service/src/main/java/org/wildfly/service/Lifecycle.java:24-27] The default `isStopped()` implementation returns `!isStarted()`, which means during `STARTING` and `STOPPING` states (defined in `Lifecycle.State`), `isStopped()` returns `true`. Both BlockingLifecycle and NonBlockingLifecycle override this to check for `State.STOPPED` specifically, so the default is never used — but it's semantically incorrect and could mislead future implementors. Consider making `isStopped()` abstract or documenting that implementations must override it.
- [service/src/main/java/org/wildfly/service/capture/ServiceValueRegistry.java:33] `.requires(dependency)` is redundant: `BlockingBuilder.of(dependency)` already internally calls `.requires(dependency)` (see ServiceInstaller.BlockingBuilder.of() factory method). Not harmful, but misleading. Remove the redundant `.requires(dependency)` call.
- [service/src/main/java/org/wildfly/service/Installer.java:BlockingValueService.stop()] The `LOGGER.warn(e.getLocalizedMessage(), e)` call is unusual — typically `LOGGER.warnf("Failed to stop lifecycle: %s", e)` or a logger method key is preferred. Consider using a more descriptive log message.

SUMMARY:
This PR fixes a bug where Topic GC incorrectly disconnects active replication producers, causing replication backlog to grow. The fix introduces an idle-replicator disconnect mechanism (configurable via `brokerReplicationInactiveThresholdSeconds`) that only disconnects replicators with zero backlog and no recent traffic, while preventing GC from triggering when replicators are still connected.

POINTS:
- [pulsar-broker/src/main/java/org/apache/pulsar/broker/service/AbstractTopic.java:691-702] `disconnectReplicatorsIfNoTrafficAndBacklog()` explicitly filters on `instanceof PersistentReplicator`. However, `NonPersistentReplicator.sendMessage()` (line 97 in the diff) now sets `latestPublishTime = System.currentTimeMillis()` — this tracking is added but never consumed for NonPersistentReplicator since it lacks the `disconnectIfNoTrafficAndBacklog()` method. This is dead code. Consider either: (a) adding the idle-disconnect support to NonPersistentReplicator for consistency, or (b) removing the `latestPublishTime` assignment in `NonPersistentReplicator.sendMessage()`.

- [pulsar-broker/src/main/java/org/apache/pulsar/broker/service/AbstractReplicator.java:387-392] The `closeProducerAsync()` method no longer calls `disableReplicatorRead()` after disconnecting. Combined with the removal of `cursor.rewind()` from `setProducerAndTriggerReadEntries()`, this means the cursor remains active and does not rewind after an idle disconnect. This is correct only if disconnect always happens with backlog=0. Consider adding an assert or debug log: `assert backlog == 0 : "disconnect should never be called with backlog > 0"` to document and enforce this invariant.

- [pulsar-broker/src/test/java/org/apache/pulsar/broker/service/OneWayReplicatorTest.java:261-267] The `@DataProvider` for `testDisconnectAndReconnectReplicator` contains Chinese comments (e.g., `// TODO terminate 的判断逻辑搬运到 replicator 中。`). These should be translated to English before merging. Additionally, lines 272-273 have Chinese text: `// resume 的判断逻辑可言在 scheduled task 中，也可以在 cursor pending read 中。`

- [pulsar-broker/src/test/java/org/apache/pulsar/broker/service/OneWayReplicatorTest.java:250-440] The `testDisconnectAndReconnectReplicator` test uses `Thread.sleep(100_000)` (100 seconds) to wait for disconnect, with `timeOut = 240 * 1000`. This is fragile — consider using `Awaitility.await().untilAsserted()` with a predicate checking `!persistentReplicator.isConnected()` instead of a fixed sleep. Also, the commented-out verification blocks (lines 265-270, 274-285) should either be enabled or removed to avoid confusion.

- [pulsar-broker/src/main/java/org/apache/pulsar/broker/service/persistent/PersistentReplicator.java:281-301] `disconnectIfNoTrafficAndBacklog()` checks `state != Started` but uses direct field access (`state != Started`) rather than the volatile updater. The `state` field is declared `volatile` with `AtomicReferenceFieldUpdater`, so direct reads are technically safe, but for consistency with the rest of the class, consider using `STATE_UPDATER.get(this) != Started`.

- [pulsar-broker/src/main/java/org/apache/pulsar/broker/service/persistent/PersistentReplicator.java:391-440] The new `replicateEntries()` method calls `retryReplicateEntries.run()` which schedules a retry on `ml.getScheduledExecutor()`. This introduces a 100ms delay when the replicator is in `Disconnected` or `Starting` state. If there's sustained high message production rate, this delay could cause cursor read positions to lag. The existing `readFailureBackoff.reduceToHalf()` is called before `replicateEntries` (line ~380), but this retry is a separate path. Consider whether this retry should use the existing backoff mechanism for consistency.

- [pulsar-broker/src/main/java/org/apache/pulsar/broker/service/BrokerService.java:741-744] The new `checkInactiveReplication()` scheduled task runs at the same interval as `checkGC()` (`brokerDeleteInactiveTopicsFrequencySeconds`). It's guarded by `getBrokerReplicationInactiveThresholdSeconds() > 0`, but the schedule is set up during startup only. If the threshold is dynamically changed from 0 to a positive value, the scheduler won't be started. Consider rescheduling dynamically when the config changes, or document that this setting is not truly dynamic despite the `@FieldContext(dynamic = true)` annotation on the adjacent config.

- [pulsar-broker/src/test/java/org/apache/pulsar/broker/service/OneWayReplicatorUsingGlobalPartitionedTest.java:87-94, OneWayReplicatorUsingGlobalZKTest.java:827-833] Both subclass tests have `testDisconnectAndReconnectReplicator` marked with `@Test(enabled = false)`. Since the parent test in `OneWayReplicatorTest` is enabled and these are just delegating calls, they should either be enabled (if the test passes in these contexts) or removed entirely to avoid dead test code.

- [pulsar-broker/src/main/java/org/apache/pulsar/broker/service/persistent/GeoPersistentReplicator.java:146, ShadowReplicator.java:64] Both subclasses renamed `replicateEntries` to `doReplicateEntries`. Verified that no other classes in the codebase override `replicateEntries` — the refactoring is complete. No other callers of the old method signature were found.

SUMMARY:
This PR adds an XML Catalogs Preference page to configure custom XML catalog files, wiring them into the LemMinX language server initialization options. The implementation introduces preference pages, an initializer, and property-change notification to push catalog updates to the running language server.

POINTS:
- [org.eclipse.wildwebdeveloper.xml/src/org/eclipse/wildwebdeveloper/xml/internal/XMLLanguageServer.java:88] **PropertyChangeListener registered but never removed.** `store.addPropertyChangeListener(this)` is called in the constructor, but there is no corresponding `store.removePropertyChangeListener(this)` anywhere. Although `XMLLanguageServer` is declared `singleton="true"` in `plugin.xml` (line 72) which reduces the severity, this is still a resource leak if the bundle is refreshed or the singleton guarantee ever changes. Consider overriding `dispose()` on `ProcessStreamConnectionProvider` (if available) or removing the listener when the bundle stops.

- [org.eclipse.wildwebdeveloper.xml/src/org/eclipse/wildwebdeveloper/xml/internal/XMLLanguageServer.java:197-200] **Diverges from established pattern for config changes.** All other language servers in this repo (YAML, CSS, HTML, JSON) push config changes by overriding `handleMessage(Message, LanguageServer, URI)` and using the `languageServer` parameter directly (`languageServer.getWorkspaceService().didChangeConfiguration(params)`). This PR instead implements `IPropertyChangeListener` and calls `LanguageServiceAccessor.getActiveLanguageServers(null)` to discover servers at runtime — a pattern with no precedent in this codebase. Consider aligning with the `handleMessage` pattern used by `YAMLLanguageServer`, which checks for preference changes on each message and pushes them directly.

- [org.eclipse.wildwebdeveloper.xml/src/org/eclipse/wildwebdeveloper/xml/internal/XMLLanguageServer.java:198] **`LanguageServiceAccessor.getActiveLanguageServers(null)` — `null` document argument is untested in this repo.** The only existing call to `LanguageServiceAccessor` (in `TestTypeScript.java:75`) passes a non-null `IDocument`. Passing `null` to `getActiveLanguageServers()` may return unexpected results or an empty collection depending on lsp4e implementation. Verify this works as intended, or consider the `handleMessage` callback approach which avoids server discovery entirely.

- [org.eclipse.wildwebdeveloper.xml/src/org/eclipse/wildwebdeveloper/xml/internal/XMLLanguageServer.java:51] **Static `lemminxDefinition` initialized from `LanguageServersRegistry` at class-load time may be null.** `LanguageServersRegistry.getInstance().getDefinition("org.eclipse.wildwebdeveloper.xml")` executes during static initialization, but the registry may not yet have registered the language server definition. If `lemminxDefinition` is null, the `propertyChange` filter at line 199 will silently do nothing (and possibly NPE on `.equals()`). Consider lazy initialization or null-checking.

- [org.eclipse.wildwebdeveloper.xml/src/org/eclipse/wildwebdeveloper/xml/internal/ui/preferences/XMLPreferenceInitializer.java:21] **Typo in constant name:** `XML_PREFERENCES_CATAGLOGS` should be `XML_PREFERENCES_CATALOGS` (note the extra "GA" — "CATAGLOGS" vs "CATALOGS"). This affects all references in `XMLLanguageServer.java:192` as well.

- [org.eclipse.wildwebdeveloper.xml/src/org/eclipse/wildwebdeveloper/xml/internal/XMLLanguageServer.java:191] **Remove auto-generated TODO stub comment:** `// TODO Auto-generated method stub` should be removed from the `propertyChange` method.

- [org.eclipse.wildwebdeveloper.xml/src/org/eclipse/wildwebdeveloper/xml/internal/ui/preferences/XMLCatalogPreferencePage.java:157] **Hardcoded editor ID string.** `"org.eclipse.ui.genericeditor.GenericEditor"` is hardcoded directly. Consider using a constant or referencing the ID from the registry to avoid brittleness if the editor ID ever changes.

SUMMARY:
This PR refactors Fluo CLI commands from positional arguments to JCommander-based option parsing, enabling Docker deployment via `-D` config overrides and console logging. The Java-side refactoring is well-structured with clean option classes (BaseOpts, ConfigOpts, CommonOpts), but the shell script (`fluo`) has multiple bugs where argument handling was not fully updated to match the new CLI format.

POINTS:
- [modules/distribution/src/main/scripts/fluo:224] **Bug: FluoExec receives wrong first argument.** The exec case passes `"$FLUO_CONN_PROPS"` as the first arg to FluoExec (`$JAVA org.apache.fluo.command.FluoExec "$FLUO_CONN_PROPS" "${@:2}"`), but the refactored FluoExec.java treats `args[0]` as `applicationName` and reads conn props from the `-Dfluo.conn.props` system property (already injected via `$JAVA`).
